items.p: You're introducing your friend's pet. Show who owns the dog. Rewrite: The dog belongs to Maria. → It's ___ dog.
items.h: Use 's to show who the dog belongs to.
items.hd: Verwende 's um zu zeigen, wem der Hund gehört.
items.e: The dog belongs to Maria → It's Maria's dog.
items.ed: Der Hund gehört Maria → It's Maria's dog.

items.p: You're labelling things in class. Use the short form with 's. Rewrite using 's: the bike of my brother → my ___
items.h: owner + 's + thing: my brother's bike.
items.hd: Besitzer + 's + Sache: my brother's bike.
items.e: The bike of my brother = my brother's bike.
items.ed: Das Fahrrad meines Bruders = my brother's bike.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Lisas Katze ist schwarz.
items.h: German: Lisas (no apostrophe). English: Lisa's (with apostrophe!).
items.hd: Deutsch: Lisas (kein Apostroph). Englisch: Lisa's (mit Apostroph!).
items.e: In English, the possessive needs an apostrophe: Lisa's cat.
items.ed: Im Englischen braucht der Genitiv einen Apostroph: Lisa's cat.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Der Geburtstag meiner Mutter ist im Mai.
items.c: My mum's birthday is in May.
a: My mum's birthday is in May.
My mother's birthday is in May.
My mom's birthday is in May.
items.h: owner + 's + thing: my mum's birthday.
items.hd: Besitzer + 's + Sache: my mum's birthday.
items.e: 'meiner Mutter' = 'my mum's'. My mum's birthday is in May.
items.ed: 'meiner Mutter' = 'my mum's'. My mum's birthday is in May.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: is / Lisa's / new / phone
items.h: Possessor + 's + noun first, then verb + adjective.
items.hd: Besitzer + 's + Nomen zuerst, dann Verb + Adjektiv.
items.e: Lisa's phone is new. Owner's thing + verb + description.
items.ed: Lisa's phone is new. Besitzer + Sache + Verb + Beschreibung.

items.p: Match the meaning: 1) Tom's happy 2) Tom's bag 3) She's nice 4) The girl's cat — a) possession b) contraction (is) c) contraction (is) d) possession
items.h: Does 's mean 'is' or 'belonging to'? Check what comes after.
items.hd: Bedeutet 's 'is' oder 'gehört'? Schau was danach kommt.
items.e: Tom's happy (=is), Tom's bag (=possession), She's nice (=is), girl's cat (=possession).
items.ed: Tom's happy (=is), Tom's bag (=Besitz), She's nice (=is), girl's cat (=Besitz).

items.p: I ___ tired. (be)
items.h: I + am/is/are?
items.hd: I + am/is/are?
items.e: I am tired. 'I' always takes 'am'.
items.ed: I am tired. 'I' nimmt immer 'am'.

items.p: The film ___ interesting. (be)
items.h: The film = it (singular).
items.hd: The film = it (Einzahl).
items.e: The film is interesting. Singular noun + is + adjective.
items.ed: The film is interesting. Einzahl-Nomen + is + Adjektiv.

items.p: They ___ very happy today. (be)
items.h: They = plural.
items.hd: They = Mehrzahl.
items.e: They are happy. Plural subject + are + adjective.
items.ed: They are happy. Mehrzahl-Subjekt + are + Adjektiv.

items.p: She ___ very ___. (be + hungry)
items.h: Subject + be + very + adjective.
items.hd: Subjekt + be + very + Adjektiv.
items.e: She is very hungry. Subject + be + (very) + adjective.
items.ed: She is very hungry. Subjekt + be + (very) + Adjektiv.

items.p: The boys ___ loud. The girl ___ quiet. (be — two gaps!)
items.h: Boys = plural (are). Girl = singular (is).
items.hd: Boys = Mehrzahl (are). Girl = Einzahl (is).
items.e: The boys are loud. The girl is quiet. Adjectives don't change — only 'be' changes.
items.ed: The boys are loud. The girl is quiet. Adjektive ändern sich nicht — nur 'be' ändert sich.

items.p: He ___ angry. He ___ very nice. (negative)
items.h: He isn't angry = He is not angry.
items.hd: He isn't angry = He is not angry.
items.e: He isn't angry. He is very nice. Use isn't for negative with he/she/it.
items.ed: He isn't angry. He is very nice. Verwende isn't für die Verneinung mit he/she/it.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: Subject + be + very + adjective.
items.hd: Subjekt + be + very + Adjektiv.
items.e: Correct order: I am very tired. 'Very' goes before the adjective.
items.ed: Richtige Reihenfolge: I am very tired. 'Very' steht vor dem Adjektiv.

items.p: The dogs are ___.
items.h: English adjectives do NOT change for singular or plural.
items.hd: Englische Adjektive ändern sich NICHT für Einzahl oder Mehrzahl.
items.e: The dogs are loud. English adjectives never change: loud (not 'louds').
items.ed: The dogs are loud. Englische Adjektive ändern sich nie: loud (nicht 'louds').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She very nice.
items.h: A verb is missing!
items.hd: Ein Verb fehlt!
items.e: You need 'be': She IS very nice.
items.ed: Du brauchst 'be': She IS very nice.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: very / am / I / tired
items.h: Where should 'very' go in the sentence?
items.hd: Wo soll 'very' im Satz stehen?
items.e: I am very tired. 'Very' goes after 'be' and before the adjective.
items.ed: I am very tired. 'Very' steht nach 'be' und vor dem Adjektiv.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The children are happys.
items.h: Do English adjectives change for plural?
items.hd: Ändern sich englische Adjektive in der Mehrzahl?
items.e: English adjectives never change: happy (not 'happys'), tall (not 'talls').
items.ed: Englische Adjektive ändern sich nie: happy (nicht 'happys').

items.p: You just had a big lunch, so you're not hungry now. Make this negative: I am hungry. → I ___ hungry.
items.h: I am → I am not / I'm not.
items.hd: I am → I am not / I'm not.
items.e: I am hungry → I am not hungry / I'm not hungry.
items.ed: I am hungry → I am not hungry / I'm not hungry.

items.p: Your friend looks upset. You want to check. Make this a question: He is sad. → ___ he sad?
items.h: Swap subject and 'be'.
items.hd: Tausche Subjekt und 'be'.
items.e: He is sad → Is he sad? Swap 'he' and 'is'.
items.ed: He is sad → Is he sad? Tausche 'he' und 'is'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich bin muede.
items.h: 'Ich bin' = 'I am'. 'muede' = 'tired'.
items.hd: 'Ich bin' = 'I am'. 'muede' = 'tired'.
items.e: Ich bin muede = I am tired.
items.ed: Ich bin muede = I am tired.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Bist du hungrig? — Nein, ich bin nicht hungrig.
items.c: Are you hungry? — No, I'm not hungry.
a: Are you hungry? — No, I'm not hungry.
Are you hungry? — No, I am not hungry.
Are you hungry? — No, I'm not hungry
items.h: 'Bist du' = 'Are you'. 'Ich bin nicht' = 'I'm not'.
items.hd: 'Bist du' = 'Are you'. 'Ich bin nicht' = 'I'm not'.
items.e: Are you hungry? — No, I'm not hungry.
items.ed: Are you hungry? — No, I'm not hungry.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: very / she / is / happy
items.h: Subject + be + very + adjective.
items.hd: Subjekt + be + very + Adjektiv.
items.e: She is very happy. Subject + be + very + adjective.
items.ed: She is very happy. Subjekt + be + very + Adjektiv.

items.p: Match the emoji to the adjective: 1) 😊 2) 😢 3) 😡 4) 😴 — a) tired b) angry c) happy d) sad
items.h: Think about what each face shows.
items.hd: Überleg, was jedes Gesicht zeigt.
items.e: 😊=happy, 😢=sad, 😡=angry, 😴=tired.
items.ed: 😊=happy, 😢=sad, 😡=angry, 😴=tired.

items.p: I'm happy ___ it's the weekend. (because / but)
items.h: 'Because' gives a reason.
items.hd: 'Because' gibt einen Grund an.
items.e: I'm happy because it's the weekend. 'Because' introduces the reason.
items.ed: I'm happy because it's the weekend. 'Because' leitet den Grund ein.

items.p: She is sad ___ her cat is sick.
items.h: Why is she sad? 'Because' introduces the reason.
items.hd: Warum ist sie traurig? 'Because' leitet den Grund ein.
items.e: She is sad because her cat is sick. Because + subject + verb.
items.ed: She is sad because her cat is sick. Because + Subjekt + Verb.

items.p: I like summer because ___ is warm. (it / is it)
items.h: After 'because', use normal English word order: subject + verb.
items.hd: Nach 'because' kommt normale englische Wortstellung: Subjekt + Verb.
items.e: Because it is warm. English keeps SVO order after 'because' (unlike German 'weil').
items.ed: Because it is warm. Englisch behalt SVO nach 'because' (anders als bei 'weil').

items.p: He is tired because he ___ up early. (get — past)
items.h: This happened in the past — he got up early.
items.hd: Das ist in der Vergangenheit passiert — he got up early.
items.e: He is tired because he got up early. The reason is in the past.
items.ed: He is tired because he got up early. Der Grund liegt in der Vergangenheit.

items.p: I'm happy ___ I have a dog. (because / because of)
items.h: 'Because' + clause (subject + verb). 'Because of' + noun only.
items.hd: 'Because' + Satz (Subjekt + Verb). 'Because of' + Nomen nur.
items.e: Because + clause: because I have a dog. (Not 'because of I have'.)
items.ed: Because + Satz: because I have a dog. (Nicht 'because of I have'.)

items.p: I don't like winter because it ___ cold. (be)
items.h: After 'because': subject + verb + adjective (English SVO order).
items.hd: Nach 'because': Subjekt + Verb + Adjektiv (englische SVO-Ordnung).
items.e: Because it is cold. NOT 'because cold it is' (German word order).
items.ed: Because it is cold. NICHT 'because cold it is' (deutsche Wortstellung).

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I like dogs because they are friendly.
a: I like dogs because they are friendly.
ds: I like dogs because friendly they are.
I like dogs because of they are friendly.
I like dogs because are they friendly.
items.h: After 'because', use normal SVO word order.
items.hd: Nach 'because' kommt normale SVO-Wortstellung.
items.e: Because they are friendly. SVO order after 'because' in English.
items.ed: Because they are friendly. SVO-Ordnung nach 'because' auf Englisch.

items.p: She is excited ___.
items.c: because she has a new bike
a: because she has a new bike
ds: because of she has a new bike
because has she a new bike
because a new bike she has
items.h: because + subject + verb + object.
items.hd: because + Subjekt + Verb + Objekt.
items.e: Because she has a new bike. Normal word order after 'because'.
items.ed: Because she has a new bike. Normale Wortstellung nach 'because'.

items.p: Which word introduces a REASON?
items.h: Which word answers the question 'Why?'
items.hd: Welches Wort beantwortet die Frage 'Warum?'
items.e: 'Because' answers 'why?' and introduces a reason.
items.ed: 'Because' beantwortet 'warum?' und leitet einen Grund ein.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: I / summer / like / because / warm / it / is
items.c: I like summer because it is warm.
a: I like summer because it is warm.
I like summer because it is warm
items.h: Check the word order after 'because'.
items.hd: Pruefe die Wortstellung nach 'because'.
items.e: After 'because', use English SVO: because it is warm (not 'warm it is').
items.ed: Nach 'because' englische SVO-Ordnung: because it is warm (nicht 'warm it is').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I'm happy because of I have a dog.
items.c: I'm happy because I have a dog.
a: I'm happy because I have a dog.
items.h: 'Because' + clause. 'Because of' + noun only.
items.hd: 'Because' + Satz. 'Because of' + nur Nomen.
items.e: Because + clause (I have a dog). Not 'because of' + clause.
items.ed: Because + Satz (I have a dog). Nicht 'because of' + Satz.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: I / like / don't / maths / because / it / difficult / is
items.c: I don't like maths because it is difficult.
a: I don't like maths because it is difficult.
I don't like maths because it is difficult
items.h: English word order: subject + verb + adjective (even after 'because').
items.hd: Englische Wortstellung: Subjekt + Verb + Adjektiv (auch nach 'because').
items.e: Because it is difficult. German puts verb at end after 'weil'; English doesn't.
items.ed: Because it is difficult. Deutsch stellt das Verb nach 'weil' ans Ende; Englisch nicht.

items.p: Join the sentences: I'm tired. I got up early. → I'm tired because ___.
a: I'm tired because I got up early.
I'm tired because I got up early
I am tired because I got up early.
items.h: Use 'because' to connect the reason.
items.hd: Verwende 'because' um den Grund zu verbinden.
items.e: I'm tired because I got up early.
items.ed: I'm tired because I got up early.

items.p: Join the sentences: He likes football. It is fun. → He likes football because ___.
a: He likes football because it is fun.
He likes football because it is fun
He likes football because it's fun.
items.h: Keep normal SVO order after 'because'.
items.hd: Behalte normale SVO-Ordnung nach 'because'.
items.e: He likes football because it is fun. Normal word order after 'because'.
items.ed: He likes football because it is fun. Normale Wortstellung nach 'because'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich bin traurig, weil mein Hund krank ist.
items.c: I am sad because my dog is sick.
a: I am sad because my dog is sick.
I'm sad because my dog is sick.
items.h: Watch the word order! 'weil...krank ist' → 'because...is sick'.
items.hd: Achte auf die Wortstellung! 'weil...krank ist' → 'because...is sick'.
items.e: 'Weil' = 'because'. But word order differs: because my dog IS sick (SVO).
items.ed: 'Weil' = 'because'. Aber die Wortstellung ist anders: because my dog IS sick (SVO).

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich mag Englisch, weil es interessant ist.
items.c: I like English because it is interesting.
a: I like English because it is interesting.
I like English because it's interesting.
items.h: German: 'weil es interessant ist' (verb at end). English: 'because it IS interesting' (SVO).
items.hd: Deutsch: 'weil es interessant ist' (Verb am Ende). Englisch: 'because it IS interesting' (SVO).
items.e: Because it is interesting. English SVO after 'because', unlike German V-final after 'weil'.
items.ed: Because it is interesting. Englisch SVO nach 'because', anders als Deutsch V-End nach 'weil'.

items.p: I like my school ___ my friends are there.
items.h: Give a reason using normal word order: because + subject + verb.
items.hd: Gib einen Grund mit normaler Wortstellung: because + Subjekt + Verb.
items.e: I like my school because + reason (SVO order).
items.ed: I like my school because + Grund (SVO-Ordnung).

items.p: Complete: I like English _____ it is fun.
a: Complete: I like English because it is fun.
Complete: I like English because it is fun
Complete: I like English because it is fun
Complete: I like English because it's fun.
items.h: Which linking word gives a reason?
items.hd: Welches Bindewort gibt einen Grund an?
items.e: Use 'because' + SVO to give a reason for liking or disliking something.
items.ed: Verwende 'because' + SVO um einen Grund anzugeben.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: happy / I'm / because / is / it / sunny
items.c: I'm happy because it is sunny.
a: I'm happy because it is sunny.
I am happy because it is sunny.
I am happy because it's sunny.
items.h: Statement + because + subject + verb + adjective.
items.hd: Aussage + because + Subjekt + Verb + Adjektiv.
items.e: I'm happy because it is sunny. SVO after 'because'.
items.ed: I'm happy because it is sunny. SVO nach 'because'.

items.p: Match the feeling to the reason: 1) I'm tired because 2) I'm happy because 3) I'm sad because 4) I'm hungry because — a) I didn't eat lunch b) it's my birthday c) my cat is sick d) I got up at 5 am
items.h: Which reason makes sense for each feeling?
items.hd: Welcher Grund passt zu jedem Gefuehl?
items.e: Tired=got up early, happy=birthday, sad=cat sick, hungry=no lunch.
items.ed: Muede=frueh aufgestanden, glücklich=Geburtstag, traurig=Katze krank, hungrig=kein Mittagessen.

items.p: I have English ___ Monday. (preposition)
items.h: Days of the week use the preposition 'on'.
items.hd: Wochentage verwenden die Präposition 'on'.
items.e: On + day: on Monday, on Tuesday, etc.
items.ed: On + Tag: on Monday, on Tuesday, etc.

items.p: We have PE ___ Thursday. (preposition)
items.h: Always use 'on' with days of the week.
items.hd: Verwende immer 'on' mit Wochentagen.
items.e: We have PE on Thursday. On + day of the week.
items.ed: We have PE on Thursday. On + Wochentag.

items.p: Which day comes after Tuesday? ___
items.h: Wed-nes-day. Watch the silent letters!
items.hd: Wed-nes-day. Achte auf die stummen Buchstaben!
items.e: Wednesday has a silent 'd': W-e-d-n-e-s-d-a-y.
items.ed: Wednesday hat ein stilles 'd': W-e-d-n-e-s-d-a-y.

items.p: She goes swimming ___ Saturdays. (preposition)
items.h: 'On' is used for days, even with the plural (Saturdays).
items.hd: 'On' wird für Tage verwendet, auch in der Mehrzahl (Saturdays).
items.e: On Saturdays = every Saturday. Use 'on' + day.
items.ed: On Saturdays = jeden Samstag. Verwende 'on' + Tag.

items.p: Which day comes after Wednesday? ___
items.h: Thurs-day. It starts with 'Th'.
items.hd: Thurs-day. Es beginnt mit 'Th'.
items.e: Thursday: T-h-u-r-s-d-a-y.
items.ed: Thursday: T-h-u-r-s-d-a-y.

items.p: We don't have school ___ Sunday. (preposition + day)
items.h: Always 'on' for days: on Sunday.
items.hd: Immer 'on' für Tage: on Sunday.
items.e: We don't have school on Sunday. 'On' + day.
items.ed: We don't have school on Sunday. 'On' + Tag.

items.p: Which is the correct spelling?
items.h: Tues-day.
items.hd: Tues-day.
items.e: Tuesday: T-u-e-s-d-a-y.
items.ed: Tuesday: T-u-e-s-d-a-y.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: Which preposition goes with days of the week?
items.hd: Welche Präposition passt zu Wochentagen?
items.e: On + day: I have music on Wednesday.
items.ed: On + Tag: I have music on Wednesday.

items.p: Which day is spelled correctly?
items.h: This is one of the trickiest days to spell!
items.hd: Das ist einer der schwierigsten Tage zum Buchstabieren!
items.e: Wednesday: W-e-d-n-e-s-d-a-y. The first 'd' is silent.
items.ed: Wednesday: W-e-d-n-e-s-d-a-y. Das erste 'd' ist stumm.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have sports on tuesday.
items.h: Days of the week must always start with a capital letter.
items.hd: Wochentage müssen immer mit einem Grossbuchstaben beginnen.
items.e: Days of the week are always capitalised in English: Tuesday.
items.ed: Wochentage werden im Englischen immer grossgeschrieben: Tuesday.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have music at Monday.
items.h: Which preposition do we use with days?
items.hd: Welche Präposition verwenden wir bei Tagen?
items.e: Use 'on' with days: on Monday (not 'at Monday').
items.ed: Verwende 'on' bei Tagen: on Monday (nicht 'at Monday').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have English in Wednesday.
items.h: Which preposition do we use with days?
items.hd: Welche Präposition verwenden wir bei Wochentagen?
items.e: Two fixes: 'in' → 'on' (preposition) and 'Wendsday' → 'Wednesday' (spelling).
items.ed: Zwei Korrekturen: 'in' → 'on' (Präposition) und 'Wendsday' → 'Wednesday' (Rechtschreibung).

items.p: What day comes after Friday? ___
a: What day comes after Friday? Saturday
items.h: Friday → ?
items.hd: Friday → ?
items.e: After Friday comes Saturday.
items.ed: Nach Friday kommt Saturday.

items.p: What day comes before Thursday? ___
a: What day comes before Thursday? Wednesday
items.h: ? → Thursday.
items.hd: ? → Thursday.
items.e: Before Thursday comes Wednesday.
items.ed: Vor Thursday kommt Wednesday.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir haben am Dienstag Turnen.
a: we have got PE on Tuesday.
items.h: 'am Dienstag' = 'on Tuesday'. 'Turnen' = 'PE'.
items.hd: 'am Dienstag' = 'on Tuesday'. 'Turnen' = 'PE'.
items.e: 'am' (German) = 'on' (English) for days. We have PE on Tuesday.
items.ed: 'am' (Deutsch) = 'on' (Englisch) bei Tagen. We have PE on Tuesday.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich spiele jeden Montag Fussball.
items.h: 'jeden Montag' = 'on Mondays' (plural = every week).
items.hd: 'jeden Montag' = 'on Mondays' (Mehrzahl = jede Woche).
items.e: 'Jeden Montag' = 'on Mondays'. Plural day = regular activity.
items.ed: 'Jeden Montag' = 'on Mondays'. Tag in der Mehrzahl = regelmäßige Aktivitaet.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: on / I / maths / have / Friday
items.h: Subject + verb + subject + on + day. Or: On + day + subject + verb + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + Verb + Objekt + on + Tag. Oder: On + Tag + Subjekt + Verb + Objekt.
items.e: I have maths on Friday. / On Friday, I have maths.
items.ed: I have maths on Friday. / On Friday, I have maths.

items.p: Match the German day to the English day: 1) Montag 2) Mittwoch 3) Freitag 4) Sonntag — a) Friday b) Sunday c) Monday d) Wednesday
items.h: Mon-tag → Mon-day, Mitt-woch → Wednes-day.
items.hd: Mon-tag → Mon-day, Mitt-woch → Wednes-day.
items.e: Montag=Monday, Mittwoch=Wednesday, Freitag=Friday, Sonntag=Sunday.
items.ed: Montag=Monday, Mittwoch=Wednesday, Freitag=Friday, Sonntag=Sunday.

items.p: Maria loves ___ (she) cat very much.
items.h: Which possessive adjective goes with 'she'?
items.hd: Welcher Possessivbegleiter passt zu 'she'?
items.e: 'She' becomes 'her' as a possessive adjective: her cat = ihre Katze.
items.ed: 'She' wird zum Possessivbegleiter 'her': her cat = ihre Katze.

items.p: Tom is in ___ (he) room.
items.h: Which possessive adjective goes with 'he'?
items.hd: Welcher Possessivbegleiter passt zu 'he'?
items.e: 'He' becomes 'his' as a possessive adjective: his room = sein Zimmer.
items.ed: 'He' wird zum Possessivbegleiter 'his': his room = sein Zimmer.

items.p: We love ___ (we) school.
items.h: Which possessive adjective goes with 'we'?
items.hd: Welcher Possessivbegleiter passt zu 'we'?
items.e: 'We' becomes 'our' as a possessive adjective: our school = unsere Schule.
items.ed: 'We' wird zum Possessivbegleiter 'our': our school = unsere Schule.

items.p: The cat is playing with ___ (it) ball.
items.h: Be careful: 'its' (no apostrophe) = possession, 'it's' = it is.
items.hd: Achtung: 'its' (ohne Apostroph) = Besitz, 'it's' = it is.
items.e: 'Its' without an apostrophe shows possession. 'It's' with an apostrophe means 'it is'.
items.ed: 'Its' ohne Apostroph zeigt Besitz. 'It's' mit Apostroph bedeutet 'it is'.

items.p: Anna and Ben walk to school with ___ (they) friends.
items.h: 'They' becomes which possessive adjective?
items.hd: 'They' wird zu welchem Possessivbegleiter?
items.e: 'They' becomes 'their' as a possessive adjective. Don't confuse with 'there' (place) or 'they're' (they are).
items.ed: 'They' wird zum Possessivbegleiter 'their'. Nicht verwechseln mit 'there' (Ort) oder 'they're' (they are).

items.p: Choose the correct sentence: Tom has got a dog. ___ dog is brown.
items.h: Tom is a boy. Which word shows that something belongs to him?
items.hd: Tom ist ein Junge. Welches Wort zeigt, dass etwas ihm gehört?
items.e: For 'he' we use the possessive adjective 'his': His dog = sein Hund.
items.ed: Für 'he' verwenden wir den Possessivbegleiter 'his': His dog = sein Hund.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence about a cat and a toy:
items.h: Remember: 'its' = belonging to it, 'it's' = it is.
items.hd: Merke: 'its' = gehört ihm/ihr, 'it's' = it is.
items.e: 'Its' (without apostrophe) is the possessive form of 'it'. 'It's' means 'it is'.
items.ed: 'Its' (ohne Apostroph) ist die Besitzform von 'it'. 'It's' bedeutet 'it is'.

items.p: Lisa and Max are here. ___ mum is a teacher.
items.h: Lisa and Max = they. What shows that something belongs to 'they'?
items.hd: Lisa und Max = they. Welches Wort zeigt Besitz bei 'they'?
items.e: 'They' becomes 'their' to show possession. Be careful with the spelling — 'there' means a place!
items.ed: 'They' wird zu 'their' für Besitz. Vorsicht bei der Schreibweise — 'there' bedeutet einen Ort!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She likes he dog very much.
items.c: She likes his dog very much.
a: She likes his dog very much.
items.h: 'He' is a subject pronoun. What is the possessive form?
items.hd: 'He' ist ein Subjektpronomen. Wie heisst der Possessivbegleiter?
items.e: We need the possessive adjective 'his' (not the subject pronoun 'he') before a noun.
items.ed: Vor einem Nomen brauchen wir den Possessivbegleiter 'his' (nicht das Subjektpronomen 'he').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The dog wags it's tail when it is happy.
items.c: The dog wags its tail when it is happy.
a: The dog wags its tail when it is happy.
The dog wags its tail when it's happy.
items.h: Is 'it's' here a contraction (it is) or a possessive (belonging to it)?
items.hd: Ist 'it's' hier eine Kurzform (it is) oder ein Besitzwort (gehört ihm)?
items.e: 'Its' (no apostrophe) shows possession. 'It's' = 'it is'. The tail belongs to the dog, so we use 'its'.
items.ed: 'Its' (ohne Apostroph) zeigt Besitz. 'It's' = 'it is'. Der Schwanz gehört dem Hund, also verwenden wir 'its'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The children are playing in there garden.
items.c: The children are playing in their garden.
a: The children are playing in their garden.
items.h: Is the garden a place or does it belong to the children?
items.hd: Ist der Garten ein Ort oder gehört er den Kindern?
items.e: 'Their' (possession) not 'there' (place). The garden belongs to the children.
items.ed: 'Their' (Besitz), nicht 'there' (Ort). Der Garten gehört den Kindern.

items.p: You found a bag and want to return it. Use a possessive adjective. Rewrite: The bag belongs to Lisa. → It is ___ bag.
items.h: Lisa is 'she'. What is the possessive adjective for 'she'?
items.hd: Lisa ist 'she'. Wie heisst der Possessivbegleiter für 'she'?
items.e: Lisa = she → possessive adjective = her. It is her bag.
items.ed: Lisa = she → Possessivbegleiter = her. It is her bag.

items.p: Rewrite using a possessive adjective: The bikes belong to Tim and me. → They are ___ bikes.
items.h: Tim and me = we. What is the possessive adjective for 'we'?
items.hd: Tim und ich = we. Wie heisst der Possessivbegleiter für 'we'?
items.e: Tim and me = we → possessive adjective = our. They are our bikes.
items.ed: Tim und ich = we → Possessivbegleiter = our. They are our bikes.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das ist mein Bruder.
items.h: 'mein' = ?
items.hd: Wie heisst 'mein' auf Englisch?
items.e: 'Mein' translates to 'my' in English.
items.ed: 'Mein' wird im Englischen zu 'my'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ihre Schwester spielt Klavier. (= die Schwester von Anna und Sophie)
items.h: Anna und Sophie = they → ? sister
items.hd: 'Ihre' (Mehrzahl) wird im Englischen zu...?
items.e: 'Ihre' (for they/plural) = 'their' in English. Be careful: 'ihre' can mean 'her' or 'their' in German!
items.ed: 'Ihre' (Mehrzahl) = 'their' auf Englisch. Achtung: 'ihre' kann 'her' oder 'their' bedeuten!

items.p: ___ name is Tom. He is in my class.
items.h: Use his, her, my, or their before a noun.
items.hd: Verwende his, her, my oder their vor einem Nomen.
items.e: Use 'his' for boys/men, 'her' for girls/women, 'their' for groups. Each possessive adjective must go before a noun.
items.ed: Verwende 'his' für Buben/Männer, 'her' für Mädchen/Frauen, 'their' für Gruppen. Jeder Possessivbegleiter steht vor einem Nomen.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: is / her / This / bag
items.h: Start with 'This'.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'This'.
items.e: The correct word order is: This is her bag. The possessive adjective 'her' goes before the noun 'bag'.
items.ed: Die richtige Wortstellung ist: This is her bag. Der Possessivbegleiter 'her' steht vor dem Nomen 'bag'.

items.p: Match the subject pronoun to the correct possessive adjective: 1) I  2) he  3) she  4) they — a) their  b) my  c) her  d) his
items.h: Think about who each pronoun refers to.
items.hd: Überlege, auf wen sich jedes Pronomen bezieht.
items.e: I → my, he → his, she → her, they → their. Each subject pronoun has its own possessive adjective.
items.ed: I → my, he → his, she → her, they → their. Jedes Subjektpronomen hat seinen eigenen Possessivbegleiter.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: We like we teacher very much.
items.c: We like our teacher very much.
a: We like our teacher very much.
items.h: 'We' is a subject pronoun. What shows that the teacher belongs to 'us'?
items.hd: 'We' ist ein Subjektpronomen. Welches Wort zeigt, dass der Lehrer zu 'uns' gehört?
items.e: Before a noun, use the possessive adjective 'our' (not the subject pronoun 'we').
items.ed: Vor einem Nomen verwendet man den Possessivbegleiter 'our' (nicht das Subjektpronomen 'we').

items.p: I ___ (can / swim) very well.
items.h: After 'can', use the base form of the verb (no 'to', no '-s').
items.hd: Nach 'can' kommt die Grundform des Verbs (ohne 'to', ohne '-s').
items.e: After 'can', always use the base form: can + swim (not 'can to swim' or 'can swims').
items.ed: Nach 'can' kommt immer die Grundform: can + swim (nicht 'can to swim' oder 'can swims').

items.p: She ___ play the guitar..
items.h: 'Can' stays the same for ALL persons — no -s for he/she/it!
items.hd: 'Can' bleibt für ALLE Personen gleich — kein -s bei he/she/it!
items.e: 'Can' never changes — it is the same for I, you, he, she, it, we, they.
items.ed: 'Can' ändert sich nie — es ist für alle Personen gleich.

items.p: My little brother ___ (not / can / ride) a bike yet.
items.h: The negative of 'can' is 'can't' or 'cannot'. Don't use 'don't'!
items.hd: Die Verneinung von 'can' ist 'can't' oder 'cannot'. Nicht 'don't' verwenden!
items.e: Modals like 'can' form the negative by adding 'not': can't / cannot. Never use 'don't can'.
items.ed: Modalverben wie 'can' werden mit 'not' verneint: can't / cannot. Nie 'don't can' verwenden.

items.p: ___ your sister ___ (can / speak) French?
items.h: To make a question with 'can', put 'Can' at the beginning.
items.hd: Um eine Frage mit 'can' zu bilden, stelle 'Can' an den Anfang.
items.e: Questions with 'can': Can + subject + base verb? No 'do/does' needed!
items.ed: Fragen mit 'can': Can + Subjekt + Grundform? Kein 'do/does' nötig!

items.p: He ___ (can / run) fast, but he ___ (not / can / swim).
items.h: 'Can' never changes. The negative is 'can't'.
items.hd: 'Can' ändert sich nie. Die Verneinung ist 'can't'.
items.e: 'Can' is the same for all persons and never takes -s. Negative: can't (not 'don't can').
items.ed: 'Can' ist für alle Personen gleich und bekommt nie -s. Verneinung: can't (nicht 'don't can').

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
ds: She can to play the piano.
items.h: After 'can': no -s on 'can', no 'to', no -s on the verb.
items.hd: Nach 'can': kein -s an 'can', kein 'to', kein -s am Verb.
items.e: 'Can' + base form. No changes at all: She can play (not cans, not plays, not to play).
items.ed: 'Can' + Grundform. Keine Änderungen: She can play (nicht cans, nicht plays, nicht to play).

items.p: Which is the correct negative sentence?
ds: I don't can dance very well.
I no can dance very well.
I can't to dance very well.
items.h: The negative of 'can' is 'can't'. You don't need 'don't' or 'to'.
items.hd: Die Verneinung von 'can' ist 'can't'. Du brauchst kein 'don't' oder 'to'.
items.e: Modal verbs form negatives without 'do': can → can't (not 'don't can').
items.ed: Modalverben werden ohne 'do' verneint: can → can't (nicht 'don't can').

items.p: Which question is correct?
items.h: Questions with 'can': Can + subject + base verb?
items.hd: Fragen mit 'can': Can + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: To form a question with 'can', invert: Can + subject + base verb? No 'do' needed.
items.ed: Frage mit 'can': Can + Subjekt + Grundform? Kein 'do' nötig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He cans ride a bike.
items.h: Does 'can' ever change for different persons?
items.hd: Ändert sich 'can' je nach Person?
items.e: 'Can' never adds -s. It is the same for all persons: I can, he can, she can, they can.
items.ed: 'Can' bekommt nie -s. Es ist für alle Personen gleich: I can, he can, she can, they can.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I can to speak English and German.
items.c: I can speak English and German.
a: I can speak English and German.
items.h: Do we need 'to' after 'can'?
items.hd: Brauchen wir 'to' nach 'can'?
items.e: After 'can', use the base form WITHOUT 'to': I can speak (not 'I can to speak').
items.ed: Nach 'can' kommt die Grundform OHNE 'to': I can speak (nicht 'I can to speak').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She don't can cook.
items.h: How do you make 'can' negative? You don't need 'don't'.
items.hd: Wie verneint man 'can'? Man braucht kein 'don't'.
items.e: Modal verbs form the negative directly: can't / cannot. Never use 'don't can'.
items.ed: Modalverben werden direkt verneint: can't / cannot. Nie 'don't can' verwenden.

items.p: Your little brother says he can dance, but he really can't! Make this sentence negative: I can dance. → I ___.
items.h: The negative of 'can' is 'can't' or 'cannot'.
items.hd: Die Verneinung von 'can' ist 'can't' oder 'cannot'.
items.e: To make 'can' negative, add 'not': can + not = can't / cannot.
items.ed: Um 'can' zu verneinen, fuege 'not' hinzu: can + not = can't / cannot.

items.p: You want to find out if Tom can play chess for the school team. Make this a question: Tom can play chess. → ___ Tom ___?
a: Can Tom play chess? Tom Can Tom play chess??
Can Tom play chess? Tom Can Tom play chess?
Can Tom play chess? Tom Can Tom play chess?
Can Tom play chess? Tom Can Tom play chess
items.h: Put 'Can' at the start to make a question.
items.hd: Stelle 'Can' an den Anfang, um eine Frage zu bilden.
items.e: For 'can' questions, put 'can' before the subject: Can + Tom + play?
items.ed: Bei Fragen mit 'can' steht 'can' vor dem Subjekt: Can + Tom + play?

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mein Bruder kann gut Fussball spielen.
items.c: My brother can play football well.
a: My brother can play football well.
My brother can play soccer well.
items.h: 'kann' = can. After 'can', use the base form.
items.hd: 'kann' = can. Nach 'can' kommt die Grundform.
items.e: 'Kann' = 'can'. After 'can', use the base form: can play (not 'can plays' or 'can to play').
items.ed: 'Kann' = 'can'. Nach 'can' kommt die Grundform: can play (nicht 'can plays' oder 'can to play').

items.p: 🇩🇪 Kannst du Klavier spielen?
items.h: Questions with 'can': Can + subject + base verb?
items.hd: Fragen mit 'can': Can + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: 'Kannst du' = 'Can you'. Remember: Can + you + play.
items.ed: 'Kannst du' = 'Can you'. Merke: Can + you + play.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: ride / she / a bike / can
items.h: Start with the subject, then 'can', then the verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt, dann 'can', dann das Verb.
items.e: Word order: Subject + can + base verb + object. She can ride a bike.
items.ed: Wortstellung: Subjekt + can + Grundform + Objekt. She can ride a bike.

items.p: Match the sentence halves: 1) She can  2) Can you  3) They can't  4) I can't — a) play chess?  b) come tomorrow.  c) sing very well.  d) ride a horse.
items.h: Look at whether each sentence is positive, negative, or a question.
items.hd: Schau, ob jeder Satz positiv, negativ oder eine Frage ist.
items.e: She can sing very well. Can you play chess? They can't come tomorrow. I can't ride a horse.
items.ed: She can sing very well. Can you play chess? They can't come tomorrow. I can't ride a horse.

items.p: She ___ (play) tennis every Saturday.
items.h: She = 3rd person. What happens to the verb?
items.hd: She = 3. Person. Was passiert mit dem Verb?
items.e: He/she/it + verb-s: She plays. Regular verbs just add -s.
items.ed: He/she/it + Verb-s: She plays. regelmäßige Verben bekommen einfach -s.

items.p: He ___ (watch) TV every evening.
items.h: Verbs ending in -ch add -es for he/she/it.
items.hd: Verben auf -ch bekommen -es bei he/she/it.
items.e: Verbs ending in -ch, -sh, -s, -x, -o add -es: watch → watches.
items.ed: Verben auf -ch, -sh, -s, -x, -o bekommen -es: watch → watches.

items.p: My dad ___ (go) to work by bus.
items.h: Verbs ending in -o add -es: go → ?
items.hd: Verben auf -o bekommen -es: go → ?
items.e: Verbs ending in -o add -es: go → goes, do → does.
items.ed: Verben auf -o bekommen -es: go → goes, do → does.

items.p: She ___ (carry) her books to school every day.
items.h: Verbs ending in consonant + y: change y to -ies.
items.hd: Verben auf Konsonant + y: ändere y zu -ies.
items.e: Consonant + y → -ies: carry → carries, study → studies, try → tries.
items.ed: Konsonant + y → -ies: carry → carries, study → studies, try → tries.

items.p: We ___ (like) pizza and they ___ (like) pasta.
items.h: Only he/she/it gets -s. What about 'we' and 'they'?
items.hd: Nur he/she/it bekommt -s. Was ist mit 'we' und 'they'?
items.e: We and they use the base form (no -s). Only he/she/it adds -s.
items.ed: We und they verwenden die Grundform (ohne -s). Nur he/she/it bekommt -s.

items.p: My sister ___ to school every day.
items.h: My sister = she (3rd person). What happens to the verb?
items.hd: My sister = she (3. Person). Was passiert mit dem Verb?
items.e: My sister = she → 3rd person singular → walks (add -s).
items.ed: My sister = she → 3. Person Einzahl → walks (mit -s).

items.p: She ___ very hard for her tests.
items.h: Study ends in consonant + y. What is the rule?
items.hd: Study endet auf Konsonant + y. Wie lautet die Regel?
items.e: Consonant + y → ies: study → studies (not studys).
items.ed: Konsonant + y → ies: study → studies (nicht studys).

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: Every day she eats an apple.
a: Every day she eats an apple.
ds: Every day eats she an apple.
Every day she eat an apple.
She every day eats an apple.
items.h: English keeps SVO order even when a time expression comes first.
items.hd: Englisch behaelt die SVO-Wortstellung, auch wenn eine Zeitangabe am Anfang steht.
items.e: English word order: (Time) + Subject + Verb. NOT like German where the verb comes second after time.
items.ed: Englische Wortstellung: (Zeit) + Subjekt + Verb. NICHT wie im Deutschen, wo das Verb nach der Zeitangabe an 2. Stelle steht.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She drink milk every morning.
items.h: She = 3rd person singular. What does the verb need?
items.hd: She = 3. Person Einzahl. Was braucht das Verb?
items.e: He/she/it needs -s on the verb: She drinks (not 'She drink').
items.ed: He/she/it braucht -s am Verb: She drinks (nicht 'She drink').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He gos to school by bus.
items.c: He goes to school by bus.
a: He goes to school by bus.
items.h: Verbs ending in -o add -es, not just -s.
items.hd: Verben auf -o bekommen -es, nicht nur -s.
items.e: Go → goes (not 'gos'). Verbs ending in -o add -es.
items.ed: Go → goes (nicht 'gos'). Verben auf -o bekommen -es.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: morning / Every / walks / she / to / school
items.c: Every morning she walks to school.
a: Every morning she walks to school.
Every morning she walks to school
items.h: In English, the subject comes before the verb, even after a time expression.
items.hd: Im Englischen steht das Subjekt vor dem Verb, auch nach einer Zeitangabe.
items.e: English keeps SVO order: Every morning + she (S) + walks (V). German V2 word order does not apply in English.
items.ed: Englisch behaelt die SVO-Reihenfolge: Every morning + she (S) + walks (V). Die deutsche V2-Regel gilt nicht im Englischen.

items.p: Change the subject to 'he': I play football every day. → He ___ football every day.
items.h: When the subject changes to 'he', what happens to the verb?
items.hd: Wenn das Subjekt zu 'he' wird, was passiert mit dem Verb?
items.e: I play → He plays. Third person singular adds -s to the verb.
items.ed: I play → He plays. Die 3. Person Einzahl bekommt -s am Verb.

items.p: Change the subject to 'she': I wash the dishes. → She ___ the dishes.
items.h: Wash ends in -sh. What spelling rule applies for he/she/it?
items.hd: Wash endet auf -sh. Welche Rechtschreibregel gilt für he/she/it?
items.e: Verbs ending in -sh add -es: wash → washes.
items.ed: Verben auf -sh bekommen -es: wash → washes.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er spielt jeden Tag Fussball.
items.h: Er = He. Don't forget the -s on the verb!
items.hd: Er = He. Vergiss nicht das -s am Verb!
items.e: He plays (with -s!) football every day. Time expressions usually go at the end in English.
items.ed: He plays (mit -s!) football every day. Zeitangaben stehen im Englischen meist am Ende.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Jeden Morgen geht sie in die Schule.
items.c: Every morning she goes to school.
a: Every morning she goes to school.
She goes to school every morning.
items.h: Be careful with the word order! In English: (time) + subject + verb.
items.hd: Vorsicht mit der Wortstellung! Auf Englisch: (Zeit) + Subjekt + Verb.
items.e: English: Every morning + she (subject) + goes (verb). NOT 'Every morning goes she' (that's German V2 word order).
items.ed: Englisch: Every morning + she (Subjekt) + goes (Verb). NICHT 'Every morning goes she' (das waere deutsche V2-Wortstellung).

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: to / every / she / school / walks / day
items.c: She walks to school every day.
a: She walks to school every day.
Every day she walks to school.
items.h: Start with the subject. Time expression can go at the end or the beginning.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt. Die Zeitangabe kann am Ende oder am Anfang stehen.
items.e: She walks to school every day. OR Every day she walks to school. (SVO word order!)
items.ed: She walks to school every day. ODER Every day she walks to school. (SVO-Wortstellung!)

items.p: Match the verb to its correct 3rd person form: 1) go  2) watch  3) study  4) play — a) watches  b) plays  c) studies  d) goes
items.h: Think about the spelling rules: -o → -es, -ch → -es, consonant + y → -ies, regular → -s.
items.hd: Denke an die Rechtschreibregeln: -o → -es, -ch → -es, Konsonant + y → -ies, normal → -s.
items.e: go → goes (-o → -es), watch → watches (-ch → -es), study → studies (y → -ies), play → plays (regular -s).
items.ed: go → goes (-o → -es), watch → watches (-ch → -es), study → studies (y → -ies), play → plays (normales -s).

items.p: She has got ___ friends at school..
items.h: The full expression is: a + lot + of + noun.
items.hd: Der vollstaendige Ausdruck ist: a + lot + of + Nomen.
items.e: 'A lot of' is a fixed expression meaning 'many/much'. Don't mix it with 'lots of'.
items.ed: 'A lot of' ist eine feste Wendung und bedeutet 'viel/viele'. Nicht mit 'lots of' mischen.

items.p: There are ___ trees in the park..
items.h: 'Lots of' means the same as 'a lot of'. Don't forget the 'of'!
items.hd: 'Lots of' bedeutet dasselbe wie 'a lot of'. Vergiss nicht das 'of'!
items.e: 'Lots of' is the informal version of 'a lot of'. Both need 'of' before the noun.
items.ed: 'Lots of' ist die informelle Version von 'a lot of'. Beide brauchen 'of' vor dem Nomen.

items.p: My brother eats ___ fruit every day..
items.h: 'A lot of' works with both countable and uncountable nouns.
items.hd: 'A lot of' funktioniert mit zaehlbaren und unzaehlbaren Nomen.
items.e: 'A lot of' can be used with uncountable nouns (fruit, water) and countable plurals (friends, books).
items.ed: 'A lot of' kann mit unzaehlbaren Nomen (fruit, water) und zaehlbaren Mehrzahlnomen (friends, books) verwendet werden.

items.p: We have got ___ homework today.
items.h: Remember: a + lot + of + noun.
items.hd: Merke: a + lot + of + Nomen.
items.e: 'Homework' is uncountable. Use 'a lot of homework' (not 'many homeworks').
items.ed: 'Homework' ist unzaehlbar. Verwende 'a lot of homework' (nicht 'many homeworks').

items.p: He drinks ___ water, but he doesn't drink ___ juice.
items.c: a lot of ... a lot of
a: a lot of ... a lot of
ds: a lots of ... a lots of
items.h: 'A lot of' works in both positive and negative sentences.
items.hd: 'A lot of' funktioniert in positiven und negativen Sätzen.
items.e: 'A lot of' can be used in affirmative and negative sentences with both countable and uncountable nouns.
items.ed: 'A lot of' kann in bejahten und verneinten Sätzen mit zaehlbaren und unzaehlbaren Nomen verwendet werden.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
items.c: I have a lot of books.
a: I have a lot of books.
ds: I have a lots of books.
items.h: The correct expression is 'a lot of' (three words).
items.hd: Der richtige Ausdruck ist 'a lot of' (drei Wörter).
items.e: 'A lot of' is always three words: a + lot + of. 'Lots of' is two words: lots + of.
items.ed: 'A lot of' besteht immer aus drei Wörtern: a + lot + of. 'Lots of' aus zwei: lots + of.

items.p: Which TWO sentences are correct?
items.c: She has a lot of friends. / She has lots of friends.
a: She has a lot of friends. / She has lots of friends.
ds: She has a lots of friends.
items.h: There are two common expressions for 'viele' in English. Both are correct here.
items.hd: Es gibt zwei gängige Ausdrücke für 'viele' im Englischen.
items.e: Both 'a lot of' and 'lots of' are correct. Never mix them into 'a lots of'.
items.ed: Sowohl 'a lot of' als auch 'lots of' sind richtig. Mische sie nie zu 'a lots of'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: There are a lots of animals in the zoo.
items.c: There are a lot of animals in the zoo.
a: There are a lot of animals in the zoo.
There are lots of animals in the zoo.
items.h: Is it 'a lot of' or 'lots of'? You can't combine them!
items.hd: Heisst es 'a lot of' oder 'lots of'? Man kann sie nicht mischen!
items.e: Either 'a lot of' or 'lots of' — never 'a lots of'. These are two separate expressions.
items.ed: Entweder 'a lot of' oder 'lots of' — nie 'a lots of'. Das sind zwei verschiedene Ausdrücke.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She has a lot friends in her class.
items.c: She has a lot of friends in her class.
a: She has a lot of friends in her class.
She has lots of friends in her class.
items.h: Something is missing between 'lot' and 'friends'.
items.hd: Zwischen 'lot' und 'friends' fehlt etwas.
items.e: 'A lot of' always needs 'of' before the noun. German 'viele' is one word, but English needs three: a lot of.
items.ed: 'A lot of' braucht immer 'of' vor dem Nomen. Deutsch 'viele' ist ein Wort, aber Englisch braucht drei: a lot of.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I eat lot of fruit every day.
items.c: I eat a lot of fruit every day.
a: I eat a lot of fruit every day.
I eat lots of fruit every day.
items.h: Something is missing before 'lot'.
items.hd: Vor 'lot' fehlt etwas.
items.e: 'A lot of' needs the article 'a': a lot of fruit (not 'lot of fruit').
items.ed: 'A lot of' braucht den Artikel 'a': a lot of fruit (nicht 'lot of fruit').

items.p: Rewrite using 'a lot of': She has many books. → She has ___ books.
a: She has a lot of books.
She has a lot of books
items.h: 'Many' and 'a lot of' have a similar meaning.
items.hd: 'Many' und 'a lot of' haben eine aehnliche Bedeutung.
items.e: 'Many books' = 'a lot of books' = 'lots of books'. All three are correct.
items.ed: 'Many books' = 'a lot of books' = 'lots of books'. Alle drei sind richtig.

items.p: Rewrite using 'lots of': We have a lot of homework. → We have ___ homework.
items.h: 'Lots of' is the informal version of 'a lot of'.
items.hd: 'Lots of' ist die informelle Version von 'a lot of'.
items.e: 'A lot of' = 'lots of'. Both mean the same and work with countable and uncountable nouns.
items.ed: 'A lot of' = 'lots of'. Beide bedeuten dasselbe und funktionieren mit zaehlbaren und unzaehlbaren Nomen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Es gibt viele Kinder im Park.
items.c: There are a lot of children in the park.
a: There are a lot of children in the park.
There are lots of children in the park.
There are a lot of kids in the park.
There are lots of kids in the park.
items.h: 'viele' = 'a lot of' or 'lots of'. Don't forget 'of'!
items.hd: 'viele' = 'a lot of' oder 'lots of'. Vergiss nicht 'of'!
items.e: German 'viele' = English 'a lot of' or 'lots of'. Always include 'of' before the noun.
items.ed: Deutsch 'viele' = Englisch 'a lot of' oder 'lots of'. Immer 'of' vor dem Nomen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir haben viel Wasser, aber nicht viel Saft.
items.c: We have a lot of water, but not a lot of juice.
a: We have a lot of water, but not a lot of juice.
We have lots of water, but not lots of juice.
We have a lot of water but not a lot of juice.
we've a lot of water, but not a lot of juice.
we've lots of water, but not lots of juice.
we've a lot of water but not a lot of juice.
We have lots of water but not lots of juice.
we've lots of water but not lots of juice.
items.h: 'viel' (uncountable) = 'a lot of' in English.
items.hd: 'viel' (unzaehlbar) = 'a lot of' auf Englisch.
items.e: 'A lot of' works for both countable and uncountable nouns in positive and negative contexts.
items.ed: 'A lot of' funktioniert für zaehlbare und unzaehlbare Nomen in positiven und negativen Sätzen.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: of / has / a / She / friends / lot
items.c: She has a lot of friends.
a: She has a lot of friends.
items.h: Start with the subject. 'A lot of' stays together.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt. 'A lot of' bleibt zusammen.
items.e: She has a lot of friends. The expression 'a lot of' comes before the noun.
items.ed: She has a lot of friends. Der Ausdruck 'a lot of' steht vor dem Nomen.

items.p: Match the correct form to the sentence: 1) She has ___ books.  2) There is ___ water.  3) They eat ___ fruit.  4) We have ___ homework. — a) a lot of  b) a lot of  c) lots of  d) a lot of
items.h: 'A lot of' and 'lots of' both work with countable and uncountable nouns.
items.hd: 'A lot of' und 'lots of' funktionieren beide mit zaehlbaren und unzaehlbaren Nomen.
items.e: 'A lot of' and 'lots of' are interchangeable — they both mean 'viel/viele' and work with all noun types.
items.ed: 'A lot of' und 'lots of' sind austauschbar — sie bedeuten beide 'viel/viele' und funktionieren mit allen Nomentypen.

items.p: Your friend swims well. Ask about his/her ability: ___ ?
items.h: Start with 'Can' to make a yes/no question.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'Can', um eine Ja/Nein-Frage zu bilden.
items.e: Can + subject + base verb? → Can you swim?
items.ed: Can + Subjekt + Grundform? → Can you swim?

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: My cat wash its face every day.
items.c: My cat washes its face every day.
a: My cat washes its face every day.
items.h: My cat = it (3rd person singular). Wash ends in -sh.
items.hd: My cat = it (3. Person Einzahl). Wash endet auf -sh.
items.e: My cat = it → 3rd person. Verbs ending in -sh add -es: wash → washes.
items.ed: My cat = it → 3. Person. Verben auf -sh bekommen -es: wash → washes.

items.p: They have got ___ (a lot of / pets) and ___ (a lot of / toys) for them.
items.c: a lot of pets and a lot of toys
a: a lot of pets and a lot of toys
lots of pets and lots of toys
ds: a lots of pets and a lots of toys
a lot pets and a lot toys
lot of pets and lot of toys
items.h: Each gap needs the full expression: a + lot + of.
items.hd: Jede Luecke braucht den vollen Ausdruck: a + lot + of.
items.e: 'A lot of' must be written in full each time it appears. Never leave out 'a' or 'of'.
items.ed: 'A lot of' muss jedes Mal vollstaendig geschrieben werden. Lass nie 'a' oder 'of' weg.

items.p: She ___ (not / eat) meat.
items.h: She = 3rd person → use 'doesn't'. After 'doesn't', use the base form (no -s!).
items.hd: She = 3. Person → verwende 'doesn't'. Nach 'doesn't' kommt die Grundform (ohne -s!).
items.e: He/she/it + doesn't + base verb (no -s!): She doesn't eat (NOT 'doesn't eats').
items.ed: He/she/it + doesn't + Grundform (ohne -s!): She doesn't eat (NICHT 'doesn't eats').

items.p: They ___ (not / play) tennis on Sundays.
items.h: They = plural → use 'don't' + base form.
items.hd: They = Mehrzahl → verwende 'don't' + Grundform.
items.e: I/you/we/they + don't + base verb: They don't play.
items.ed: I/you/we/they + don't + Grundform: They don't play.

items.p: My brother ___ (not / watch) TV in the morning.
items.h: My brother = he. After 'doesn't', the verb stays in base form.
items.hd: Mein Bruder = he. Nach 'doesn't' bleibt das Verb in der Grundform.
items.e: He/she/it + doesn't + base verb: doesn't watch (NOT 'doesn't watches').
items.ed: He/she/it + doesn't + Grundform: doesn't watch (NICHT 'doesn't watches').

items.p: Tom ___ (not / go) to school on Saturdays and his sisters ___ (not / go) either.
a: does not go ... do not go
items.h: Tom = he (doesn't). His sisters = they (don't).
items.hd: Tom = he (doesn't). Seine Schwestern = they (don't).
items.e: He/she/it → doesn't. I/you/we/they → don't. Both use the base form after.
items.ed: He/she/it → doesn't. I/you/we/they → don't. Beide verwenden danach die Grundform.

items.p: Tom ___ coffee.
items.h: Tom = he. Which form do we need?
items.hd: Tom = he. Welche Form brauchen wir?
items.e: Tom = he → doesn't + base verb: doesn't drink (NOT 'doesn't drinks').
items.ed: Tom = he → doesn't + Grundform: doesn't drink (NICHT 'doesn't drinks').

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: After 'doesn't', the verb stays in base form — no -s!
items.hd: Nach 'doesn't' bleibt das Verb in der Grundform — kein -s!
items.e: She + doesn't + base verb. The -s is already 'inside' doesn't (does + not).
items.ed: She + doesn't + Grundform. Das -s steckt schon in doesn't (does + not).

items.p: Choose the correct negative sentence about 'we':
items.h: We = plural → don't (not doesn't). After 'don't', use the base form.
items.hd: We = Mehrzahl → don't (nicht doesn't). Nach 'don't' kommt die Grundform.
items.e: We + don't + base verb: We don't live. Don't add -s after 'don't'.
items.ed: We + don't + Grundform: We don't live. Kein -s nach 'don't'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He don't like vegetables.
items.h: He = 3rd person singular. Which auxiliary is correct?
items.hd: He = 3. Person Einzahl. Welches Hilfsverb ist richtig?
items.e: He/she/it needs 'doesn't' (not 'don't'): He doesn't like vegetables.
items.ed: He/she/it braucht 'doesn't' (nicht 'don't'): He doesn't like vegetables.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She doesn't plays the guitar.
a: She does not play the guitar.
items.h: After 'doesn't', does the verb need -s?
items.hd: Braucht das Verb nach 'doesn't' ein -s?
items.e: After 'doesn't', always use the base form: doesn't play (NOT 'doesn't plays'). The -s is already in 'does'.
items.ed: Nach 'doesn't' kommt immer die Grundform: doesn't play (NICHT 'doesn't plays'). Das -s steckt schon in 'does'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: My cat doesn't likes milk.
a: My cat does not like milk.
items.h: After 'doesn't', the verb must be in base form.
items.hd: Nach 'doesn't' muss das Verb in der Grundform stehen.
items.e: doesn't + base form: My cat doesn't like milk. Never add -s after doesn't.
items.ed: doesn't + Grundform: My cat doesn't like milk. Nie -s nach doesn't.

items.p: Your friend thinks you like spinach, but you don't! Make this sentence negative: I like spinach. → I ___ spinach.
items.h: For 'I', use 'don't' + base form.
items.hd: Für 'I' verwende 'don't' + Grundform.
items.e: Positive: I like → Negative: I don't like. Add 'don't' before the base verb.
items.ed: Positiv: I like → Negativ: I don't like. Fuege 'don't' vor der Grundform ein.

items.p: Tom used to play tennis but not any more. Make this sentence negative: He plays tennis. → He ___ tennis.
items.h: He → 'doesn't'. And remember: the verb loses its -s!
items.hd: He → 'doesn't'. Und denke daran: das Verb verliert sein -s!
items.e: He plays → He doesn't play. The -s moves from the verb to 'does' (does+n't).
items.ed: He plays → He doesn't play. Das -s wandert vom Verb zu 'does' (does+n't).

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich mag keinen Fisch.
items.h: 'kein' / 'nicht' in present simple = don't / doesn't + base verb.
items.hd: Wie sagt man 'kein/nicht' im Present Simple?
items.e: German 'kein/nicht' → English 'don't/doesn't' + base verb: I don't like fish.
items.ed: Deutsch 'kein/nicht' → Englisch 'don't/doesn't' + Grundform: I don't like fish.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Sie spielt nicht gerne Fussball. (= ein Mädchen)
a: She doesn't like to play football.
She does not like playing football.
She does not like to play football.
items.h: She = 3rd person → doesn't. After doesn't, use the base form!
items.hd: She = 3. Person → doesn't. Nach doesn't kommt die Grundform!
items.e: She doesn't like = sie mag nicht. Remember: doesn't + base form (no -s on the verb).
items.ed: She doesn't like = sie mag nicht. Merke: doesn't + Grundform (kein -s am Verb).

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: eat / doesn't / she / breakfast
items.h: Start with the subject. Then the negative auxiliary, then the verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt. Dann das verneinende Hilfsverb, dann das Verb.
items.e: Word order: Subject + doesn't + base verb + object. She doesn't eat breakfast.
items.ed: Wortstellung: Subjekt + doesn't + Grundform + Objekt. She doesn't eat breakfast.

items.p: Match the subject to the correct negative form: 1) I  2) She  3) They  4) He — a) doesn't like  b) don't like  c) doesn't eat  d) don't eat
items.h: I/they → don't. She/he → doesn't.
items.hd: I/they → don't. She/he → doesn't.
items.e: I/you/we/they + don't. He/she/it + doesn't. Always + base verb (no -s).
items.ed: I/you/we/they + don't. He/she/it + doesn't. Immer + Grundform (ohne -s).

items.p: I want ___ banana.
items.h: 'Banana' starts with a consonant sound /b/.
items.hd: 'Banana' beginnt mit einem Konsonantenlaut /b/.
items.e: Use 'a' before consonant sounds: a banana, a cat, a dog.
items.ed: Verwende 'a' vor Konsonantenlauten: a banana, a cat, a dog.

items.p: She eats ___ apple every day.
items.h: 'Apple' starts with a vowel sound /ae/.
items.hd: 'Apple' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut.
items.e: Use 'an' before vowel sounds: an apple, an egg, an orange.
items.ed: Verwende 'an' vor Vokallauten: an apple, an egg, an orange.

items.p: He wants ___ egg for breakfast.
items.h: 'Egg' starts with a vowel sound /e/.
items.hd: 'Egg' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut /e/.
items.e: 'Egg' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an': an egg.
items.ed: 'Egg' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut, daher verwenden wir 'an': an egg.

items.p: I want ___ orange and ___ banana.
items.h: Orange starts with a vowel sound. Banana starts with a consonant sound.
items.hd: Orange beginnt mit einem Vokallaut. Banana beginnt mit einem Konsonantenlaut.
items.e: An orange (vowel sound) + a banana (consonant sound). Check the SOUND, not just the letter!
items.ed: An orange (Vokallaut) + a banana (Konsonantenlaut). Pruefe den LAUT, nicht nur den Buchstaben!

items.p: She is ___ honest girl. She goes to ___ university.
items.h: 'Honest' has a silent 'h' (vowel sound). 'University' starts with /j/ (consonant sound).
items.hd: 'Honest' hat ein stummes 'h' (Vokallaut). 'University' beginnt mit /j/ (Konsonantenlaut).
items.e: It's about the SOUND: 'honest' → /ɒ/ vowel sound → 'an'. 'University' → /juː/ consonant sound → 'a'.
items.ed: Es geht um den LAUT: 'honest' → /ɒ/ Vokallaut → 'an'. 'University' → /juː/ Konsonantenlaut → 'a'.

items.p: She has got ___ orange cat.
items.h: Listen to the FIRST SOUND of the next word. Is it a vowel sound or consonant sound?
items.hd: Höre auf den ERSTEN LAUT des nächsten Wortes. Vokal oder Konsonant?
items.e: 'Orange' starts with a vowel sound, so use 'an': an orange cat.
items.ed: 'Orange' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut, also verwende 'an': an orange cat.

items.p: My dad is ___ teacher.
items.h: Listen to the FIRST SOUND of the next word. Is it a vowel sound or consonant sound?
items.hd: Höre auf den ERSTEN LAUT des nächsten Wortes. Vokal oder Konsonant?
items.e: In English, use 'a/an' for jobs: He is a teacher. (German says 'Er ist Lehrer' without article.)
items.ed: Im Englischen verwendet man 'a/an' bei Berufen: He is a teacher. (Im Deutschen: 'Er ist Lehrer' ohne Artikel.)

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: 'Actor' starts with a vowel sound /ae/. English needs an article before jobs.
items.hd: 'Actor' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut. Englisch braucht einen Artikel vor Berufen.
items.e: Actor starts with vowel sound → 'an actor'. English always uses a/an before jobs.
items.ed: Actor beginnt mit Vokallaut → 'an actor'. Englisch verwendet immer a/an vor Berufen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He is a actor in a film.
items.c: He is an actor in a film.
a: He is an actor in a film.
he's an actor in a film.
items.h: 'Actor' starts with a vowel sound. Which article do we need?
items.hd: 'Actor' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut. Welchen Artikel brauchen wir?
items.e: Before vowel sounds, use 'an': an actor (not 'a actor').
items.ed: Vor Vokallauten verwende 'an': an actor (nicht 'a actor').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have got dog at home.
items.c: I have got a dog at home.
a: I have got a dog at home.
I've got a dog at home.
items.h: In English, you need 'a' or 'an' before a singular countable noun.
items.hd: Im Englischen braucht man 'a' oder 'an' vor einem zaehlbaren Nomen in der Einzahl.
items.e: English requires an article before singular countable nouns. German can omit it sometimes, but English can't.
items.ed: Englisch braucht einen Artikel vor zaehlbaren Nomen in der Einzahl. Im Deutschen kann man ihn manchmal weglassen, im Englischen nicht.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She goes to an university in Vienna.
items.c: She goes to a university in Vienna.
a: She goes to a university in Vienna.
items.h: Listen to the first SOUND of 'university'. Is it a vowel sound or a consonant sound?
items.hd: Hör dir den ersten LAUT von 'university' an. Ist es ein Vokal- oder Konsonantenlaut?
items.e: 'University' starts with /juː/ — a consonant sound (like 'you'). So we use 'a', not 'an'.
items.ed: 'University' beginnt mit /juː/ — einem Konsonantenlaut (wie 'you'). Daher verwenden wir 'a', nicht 'an'.

items.p: Add 'a' or 'an': I eat ___ ice cream. → I eat ___ ice cream.
items.h: 'Ice cream' starts with a vowel sound /aɪ/.
items.hd: 'Ice cream' beginnt mit einem Vokallaut /aɪ/.
items.e: 'Ice cream' starts with vowel sound /aɪ/ → 'an ice cream'.
items.ed: 'Ice cream' beginnt mit Vokallaut /aɪ/ → 'an ice cream'.

items.p: Fill in 'a' or 'an' for each: ___ umbrella, ___ European country, ___ hour.
items.c: an umbrella, a European country, an hour
a: an umbrella, a European country, an hour
items.h: Think about the SOUND each word starts with, not the letter!
items.hd: Denke an den LAUT, mit dem jedes Wort beginnt, nicht an den Buchstaben!
items.e: Umbrella → /ʌ/ vowel → an. European → /jʊ/ consonant → a. Hour → silent h, /aʊ/ vowel → an.
items.ed: Umbrella → /ʌ/ Vokal → an. European → /jʊ/ Konsonant → a. Hour → stummes h, /aʊ/ Vokal → an.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich möchte einen Apfel und eine Banane.
items.c: I want an apple and a banana.
a: I want an apple and a banana.
I would like an apple and a banana.
items.h: Apple starts with a vowel sound → 'an'. Banana starts with a consonant → 'a'.
items.hd: Apple beginnt mit einem Vokallaut → 'an'. Banana beginnt mit einem Konsonanten → 'a'.
items.e: German 'einen/eine' = English 'a/an'. Choose based on the sound of the next word.
items.ed: Deutsch 'einen/eine' = Englisch 'a/an'. Waehle basierend auf dem Laut des nächsten Wortes.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mein Vater ist Lehrer.
items.h: In German you say 'Er ist Lehrer' (no article). In English you NEED 'a/an' before jobs!
items.hd: Im Deutschen sagt man 'Er ist Lehrer' (ohne Artikel). Im Englischen BRAUCHT man 'a/an' vor Berufen!
items.e: English uses 'a/an' before jobs: He is a teacher. German doesn't: Er ist Lehrer.
items.ed: Englisch verwendet 'a/an' vor Berufen: He is a teacher. Deutsch nicht: Er ist Lehrer.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: elephant / is / an / It
items.h: Start with the subject 'It'. 'Elephant' starts with a vowel.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt 'It'. 'Elephant' beginnt mit einem Vokal.
items.e: It is an elephant. 'An' before 'elephant' because it starts with a vowel sound.
items.ed: It is an elephant. 'An' vor 'elephant', weil es mit einem Vokallaut beginnt.

items.p: Match 'a' or 'an' to each word: 1) ___ dog  2) ___ elephant  3) ___ umbrella  4) ___ cat — a) a  b) an  c) an  d) a
items.h: Check the first sound: vowel sounds get 'an', consonant sounds get 'a'.
items.hd: Pruefe den ersten Laut: Vokallaute bekommen 'an', Konsonantenlaute bekommen 'a'.
items.e: a dog (consonant), an elephant (vowel), an umbrella (vowel), a cat (consonant).
items.ed: a dog (Konsonant), an elephant (Vokal), an umbrella (Vokal), a cat (Konsonant).

items.p: I ___ eat breakfast before school..
items.h: Frequency adverbs go BEFORE the main verb: I always eat.
items.hd: Häufigkeitsadverbien stehen VOR dem Hauptverb: I always eat.
items.e: Before main verbs: I always eat breakfast. The adverb goes between subject and verb.
items.ed: Vor Hauptverben: I always eat breakfast. Das Adverb steht zwischen Subjekt und Verb.

items.p: She is ___ happy..
items.h: After 'be': Subject + be + adverb. She is usually happy.
items.hd: Nach 'be': Subjekt + be + Adverb. She is usually happy.
items.e: After 'be', the adverb comes AFTER: She is usually happy (not 'She usually is happy').
items.ed: Nach 'be' kommt das Adverb DANACH: She is usually happy (nicht 'She usually is happy').

items.p: He ___ drinks coffee..
items.h: 'Never' goes before the main verb. 'Never' already means 'not', so don't add 'don't'!
items.hd: 'Never' steht vor dem Hauptverb. 'Never' bedeutet schon 'nicht', also kein 'don't' dazu!
items.e: He never drinks coffee. 'Never' = not ever. Don't use double negation (don't never).
items.ed: He never drinks coffee. 'Never' = nicht jemals. Keine doppelte Verneinung (don't never).

items.p: We ___ go to the cinema on Saturdays..
items.h: Frequency adverb goes before the main verb: We sometimes go.
items.hd: Häufigkeitsadverb steht vor dem Hauptverb: We sometimes go.
items.e: We sometimes go to the cinema. 'Sometimes' can also go at the beginning or end of a sentence.
items.ed: We sometimes go to the cinema. 'Sometimes' kann auch am Anfang oder Ende des Satzes stehen.

items.p: My parents are ___ tired after work..
items.h: After 'be' (are), the adverb comes AFTER: are often tired.
items.hd: Nach 'be' (are) kommt das Adverb DANACH: are often tired.
items.e: My parents are often tired. With 'be', the adverb goes AFTER: are + often + adjective.
items.ed: My parents are often tired. Bei 'be' steht das Adverb DANACH: are + often + Adjektiv.

items.p: Which sentence has the adverb in the correct position?
items.h: Frequency adverbs usually go BEFORE the main verb.
items.hd: Häufigkeitsadverbien stehen normalerweise VOR dem Hauptverb.
items.e: She always walks to school. Adverb of frequency goes between subject and main verb.
items.ed: She always walks to school. Das Häufigkeitsadverb steht zwischen Subjekt und Hauptverb.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: He is always late for school.
a: He is always late for school.
ds: He always is late for school.
He late is always for school.
Always he is late for school.
items.h: With 'be', the adverb goes AFTER: is/am/are + adverb.
items.hd: Bei 'be' steht das Adverb DANACH: is/am/are + Adverb.
items.e: He is always late. With 'be', put the adverb AFTER 'be' (not before).
items.ed: He is always late. Bei 'be' steht das Adverb NACH 'be' (nicht davor).

items.p: Choose the correct sentence with 'never':
items.h: 'Never' already means 'not ever'. You don't need 'don't' as well.
items.hd: 'Never' bedeutet schon 'nicht jemals'. Du brauchst kein 'don't' dazu.
items.e: I never eat fish. 'Never' = not ever. Don't use 'don't' + 'never' (that's double negation).
items.ed: I never eat fish. 'Never' = nicht jemals. Verwende nicht 'don't' + 'never' (doppelte Verneinung).

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: always / I / eat / breakfast / at / seven
items.c: I always eat breakfast at seven.
a: I always eat breakfast at seven.
I always eat breakfast at seven
items.h: Where does 'always' go — before or after the main verb?
items.hd: Wo steht 'always' — vor oder nach dem Hauptverb?
items.e: Before main verbs: I always eat (not 'I eat always'). This is different from German word order.
items.ed: Vor Hauptverben: I always eat (nicht 'I eat always'). Das ist anders als die deutsche Wortstellung.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: always / She / is / happy / in / the / morning
items.c: She is always happy in the morning.
a: She is always happy in the morning.
She is always happy in the morning
items.h: With 'be' (is/am/are), the adverb goes AFTER, not before.
items.hd: Bei 'be' (is/am/are) steht das Adverb DANACH, nicht davor.
items.e: She is always happy. With 'be', the adverb goes AFTER: is always (not 'always is').
items.ed: She is always happy. Bei 'be' steht das Adverb DANACH: is always (nicht 'always is').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He doesn't never drink milk.
a: He does not ever drink milk.
items.h: 'Never' and 'doesn't' are both negative. English doesn't allow double negation.
items.hd: 'Never' und 'doesn't' sind beide negativ. Englisch erlaubt keine doppelte Verneinung.
items.e: Use either 'never' alone (He never drinks) or 'doesn't ever' (He doesn't ever drink). Not both!
items.ed: Verwende entweder 'never' allein (He never drinks) oder 'doesn't ever' (He doesn't ever drink). Nicht beides!

items.p: Rewrite with the correct word order: I walk to school. (+ usually)
items.h: Put the adverb before the main verb.
items.hd: Stelle das Adverb vor das Hauptverb.
items.e: I usually walk to school. Frequency adverbs go between the subject and the main verb.
items.ed: I usually walk to school. Häufigkeitsadverbien stehen zwischen Subjekt und Hauptverb.

items.p: Your brother is always tired in the morning. Add the word. Rewrite with the correct word order: He is tired. (+ often)
items.h: With 'be', the adverb goes AFTER the verb.
items.hd: Bei 'be' steht das Adverb NACH dem Verb.
items.e: He is often tired. After 'be', the adverb comes between 'be' and the adjective.
items.ed: He is often tired. Nach 'be' steht das Adverb zwischen 'be' und dem Adjektiv.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich esse immer Fruehstueck.
items.h: 'immer' = 'always'. Put it BEFORE the main verb in English!
items.hd: 'immer' = 'always'. Stelle es im Englischen VOR das Hauptverb!
items.e: German: Ich esse immer (verb + adverb). English: I always eat (adverb + verb). Different word order!
items.ed: Deutsch: Ich esse immer (Verb + Adverb). Englisch: I always eat (Adverb + Verb). Andere Wortstellung!

items.p: 🇩🇪 Sie ist manchmal muede nach der Schule.
items.c: She is sometimes tired after school.
a: She is sometimes tired after school.
Sometimes she is tired after school.
items.h: With 'be' (is), 'sometimes' goes AFTER 'is'. Or at the beginning of the sentence.
items.hd: Bei 'be' (is) steht 'sometimes' NACH 'is'. Oder am Satzanfang.
items.e: She is sometimes tired. With 'be', the adverb goes after. 'Sometimes' can also start the sentence.
items.ed: She is sometimes tired. Bei 'be' steht das Adverb danach. 'Sometimes' kann auch am Satzanfang stehen.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: always / is / happy / she
items.h: With 'be': subject + is/am/are + adverb + adjective.
items.hd: Bei 'be': Subjekt + is/am/are + Adverb + Adjektiv.
items.e: She is always happy. With 'be', the adverb goes after 'is'.
items.ed: She is always happy. Bei 'be' steht das Adverb nach 'is'.

items.p: Match the adverb to its frequency: 1) always  2) never  3) sometimes  4) usually — a) 0%  b) 100%  c) about 50%  d) about 80%
items.h: Think of a scale from 0% (never) to 100% (always).
items.hd: Denke an eine Skala von 0% (never) bis 100% (always).
items.e: never = 0%, sometimes ≈ 50%, usually ≈ 80%, always = 100%.
items.ed: never = 0%, sometimes ≈ 50%, usually ≈ 80%, always = 100%.

items.p: Lisa ___ (not / study) French. She ___ (not / want) to learn it.
a: does not study ... does not want
items.h: Lisa = she → doesn't in both sentences. No -s on the verbs after doesn't!
items.hd: Lisa = she → doesn't in beiden Sätzen. Kein -s an den Verben nach doesn't!
items.e: Both verbs use doesn't + base form: doesn't study, doesn't want. No -s on either verb.
items.ed: Beide Verben verwenden doesn't + Grundform: doesn't study, doesn't want. Kein -s an beiden Verben.

items.p: My mum is ___ nurse and my dad is ___ engineer.
items.h: Nurse starts with /n/ (consonant). Engineer starts with /e/ (vowel). English needs a/an before jobs!
items.hd: Nurse beginnt mit /n/ (Konsonant). Engineer beginnt mit /e/ (Vokal). Englisch braucht a/an vor Berufen!
items.e: A nurse (consonant sound) + an engineer (vowel sound). In English, always use a/an before jobs.
items.ed: A nurse (Konsonantenlaut) + an engineer (Vokallaut). Im Englischen immer a/an vor Berufen verwenden.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: often / We / go / to / the / park / after / school
items.c: We often go to the park after school.
a: We often go to the park after school.
We often go to the park after school
items.h: Where should 'often' go — before or after 'go'?
items.hd: Wo soll 'often' stehen — vor oder nach 'go'?
items.e: We often go (not 'go often'). Frequency adverbs go before the main verb in English.
items.ed: We often go (nicht 'go often'). Häufigkeitsadverbien stehen im Englischen vor dem Hauptverb.

items.p: ___ she like chocolate? — Yes, she ___.
items.h: She = 3rd person → 'Does'. Short answer: Yes, she does.
items.hd: She = 3. Person → 'Does'. Kurzantwort: Yes, she does.
items.e: Does + he/she/it + base verb? Short answer: Yes, she does. / No, she doesn't.
items.ed: Does + he/she/it + Grundform? Kurzantwort: Yes, she does. / No, she doesn't.

items.p: ___ they play football after school?
items.h: They = not 3rd person singular. Which auxiliary?
items.hd: They = nicht 3. Person Einzahl. Welches Hilfsverb?
items.e: They → Do. Only he/she/it → Does.
items.ed: They → Do. Nur he/she/it → Does.

items.p: ___ he ___ (speak) English? — No, he ___.
items.h: He → Does. After 'Does', the verb stays in base form (no -s!). Short answer: No, he doesn't.
items.hd: He → Does. Nach 'Does' bleibt das Verb in der Grundform (kein -s!). Kurzantwort: No, he doesn't.
items.e: Does he speak? No, he doesn't. After 'does', always use the base form — no -s!
items.ed: Does he speak? No, he doesn't. Nach 'does' immer die Grundform — kein -s!

items.p: ___ your mum ___ (work) in an office? — No, she ___. She ___ (work) at home.
items.h: Question: Does + base form. Negative: doesn't + base form. Affirmative: she works (with -s!).
items.hd: Frage: Does + Grundform. Negativ: doesn't + Grundform. Positiv: she works (mit -s!).
items.e: Does she work? No, she doesn't. She works at home. The -s comes back in the affirmative!
items.ed: Does she work? No, she doesn't. She works at home. Das -s kommt in der bejahten Form zurueck!

items.p: Which question is correct?
items.h: After 'Does', the verb stays in base form — no -s!
items.hd: Nach 'Does' bleibt das Verb in der Grundform — kein -s!
items.e: Does + she + base verb (like, not likes!): Does she like music?
items.ed: Does + she + Grundform (like, nicht likes!): Does she like music?

items.p: _____ he _____ to school by bus?
items.h: He → Does. After 'Does', the verb is in base form.
items.hd: He → Does. Nach 'Does' steht das Verb in der Grundform.
items.e: Does he go (not goes!) to school? The -es is already 'inside' does.
items.ed: Does he go (nicht goes!) to school? Das -es steckt schon in 'does'.

items.p: Which short answer is correct? — Do they live in Vienna?
items.h: Short answer to a 'Do' question: Yes, ... do. / No, ... don't.
items.hd: Kurzantwort auf eine 'Do'-Frage: Yes, ... do. / No, ... don't.
items.e: Do they...? → Yes, they do. / No, they don't. Short answers repeat the auxiliary, not the verb.
items.ed: Do they...? → Yes, they do. / No, they don't. Kurzantworten wiederholen das Hilfsverb, nicht das Verb.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Does she likes chocolate?
items.h: After 'does', does the verb need -s?
items.hd: Braucht das Verb nach 'does' ein -s?
items.e: After 'does', always use the base form: Does she like (NOT likes).
items.ed: Nach 'does' immer die Grundform: Does she like (NICHT likes).

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Do he play tennis?
items.h: He = 3rd person singular. Which auxiliary do we need?
items.hd: He = 3. Person Einzahl. Welches Hilfsverb brauchen wir?
items.e: He/she/it needs 'Does' (not 'Do'): Does he play tennis?
items.ed: He/she/it braucht 'Does' (nicht 'Do'): Does he play tennis?

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: You like dogs? Yes, I like.
items.c: Do you like dogs? Yes, I do.
a: Do you like dogs? Yes, I do.
Do you like dogs? Yes, I do
do you like dogs yes i do
items.h: English questions need 'Do/Does' at the beginning. Short answers use the auxiliary, not the verb.
items.hd: Englische Fragen brauchen 'Do/Does' am Anfang. Kurzantworten verwenden das Hilfsverb, nicht das Verb.
items.e: Do you like dogs? (not just 'You like dogs?'). Short answer: Yes, I do (not 'Yes, I like').
items.ed: Do you like dogs? (nicht nur 'You like dogs?'). Kurzantwort: Yes, I do (nicht 'Yes, I like').

items.p: You want to find out if your new friend likes sport. Make a question: You like sport. → ___ you ___ sport?
a: Do ... like you Do ... like sport?
Do ... like you Do ... like sport
Do ... like you Do ... like sport
items.h: Put 'Do' before the subject to make a yes/no question.
items.hd: Stelle 'Do' vor das Subjekt, um eine Ja/Nein-Frage zu bilden.
items.e: You like sport. → Do you like sport? Add 'Do' at the start, keep the base form.
items.ed: You like sport. → Do you like sport? Fuege 'Do' am Anfang ein, behalte die Grundform.

items.p: Make a question: She plays the guitar. → ___ she ___ the guitar?
a: Does ... play she Does ... play the guitar?
Does ... play she Does ... play the guitar
Does ... play she Does ... play the guitar
items.h: She → Does. And the verb goes back to base form (no -s)!
items.hd: She → Does. Und das Verb kehrt zur Grundform zurueck (ohne -s)!
items.e: She plays → Does she play? The -s moves from the verb to 'does'.
items.ed: She plays → Does she play? Das -s wandert vom Verb zu 'does'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Magst du Pizza?
items.h: You → Do. 'magst' → 'like' (base form after 'do').
items.hd: You → Do. 'magst' → 'like' (Grundform nach 'do').
items.e: German inverts the verb. English adds 'Do': Do you like pizza?
items.ed: Deutsch stellt das Verb um. Englisch fuegt 'Do' hinzu: Do you like pizza?

items.p: 🇩🇪 Spielt dein Bruder gerne Fussball?
items.c: Does your brother like playing football?
a: Does your brother like playing football?
Does your brother like to play football?
items.h: Your brother = he → Does. After 'Does', use the base form!
items.hd: Dein Bruder = he → Does. Nach 'Does' kommt die Grundform!
items.e: Your brother = he → Does your brother like...? (not 'Do' or 'Does...likes').
items.ed: Dein Bruder = he → Does your brother like...? (nicht 'Do' oder 'Does...likes').

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: like / she / Does / sport / ?
items.h: Questions start with the auxiliary: Does + subject + base verb?
items.hd: Fragen beginnen mit dem Hilfsverb: Does + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: Does she like sport? Word order: Does + subject + base verb + object?
items.ed: Does she like sport? Wortstellung: Does + Subjekt + Grundform + Objekt?

items.p: Match each question to the correct short answer: 1) Do you like cats?  2) Does he play football?  3) Do they live here?  4) Does she speak French? — a) No, she doesn't.  b) Yes, I do.  c) No, they don't.  d) Yes, he does.
items.h: Match the pronoun in the question with the pronoun in the answer.
items.hd: Ordne das Pronomen in der Frage dem Pronomen in der Antwort zu.
items.e: Do you → I do. Does he → he does. Do they → they don't. Does she → she doesn't.
items.ed: Do you → I do. Does he → he does. Do they → they don't. Does she → she doesn't.

items.p: Anna likes reading. Ask a yes/no question about Anna:
items.h: Anna = she → Does. After Does, use the base form of the verb.
items.hd: Anna = she → Does. Nach Does kommt die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: Anna likes reading → Does Anna like reading? The -s moves from the verb to 'does'.
items.ed: Anna likes reading → Does Anna like reading? Das -s wandert vom Verb zu 'does'.

items.p: I like Tom. I like ___.
items.h: Tom = he. What is the object form of 'he'?
items.hd: Tom = he. Wie heisst die Objektform von 'he'?
items.e: He → him as object pronoun. I like him (not 'I like he').
items.ed: He → him als Objektpronomen. I like him (nicht 'I like he').

items.p: Can you help ___? (I)
items.h: 'I' becomes which word when it is the object?
items.hd: 'I' wird zu welchem Wort, wenn es das Objekt ist?
items.e: I → me as object pronoun. Help me (not 'help I').
