items.hd: Auch bei unregelmäßigen Verben die GRUNDFORM nach 'didn't' verwenden: didn't GO (nicht went!).
items.e: didn't + go (base). The past is already in 'didn't'. So: didn't go, NOT didn't went.
items.ed: didn't + go (Grundform). Die Vergangenheit steckt schon in 'didn't'. Also: didn't go, NICHT didn't went.

items.p: He _____ (not/eat) his lunch.
items.h: eat (base) / ate (past). After 'didn't', which one?
items.hd: eat (Grundform) / ate (Vergangenheit). Nach 'didn't', welche Form?
items.e: didn't + eat (base form). The irregular form 'ate' is only used in affirmative past.
items.ed: didn't + eat (Grundform). Die unregelmäßige Form 'ate' wird nur in der bejahten Vergangenheit verwendet.

items.p: We _____ (not/have) homework yesterday, _____ we _____ (not/do) any work at home.
items.h: After 'didn't', always use the base form: have, do (not had, did).
items.hd: Nach 'didn't' immer die Grundform verwenden: have, do (nicht had, did).
items.e: didn't have, didn't do. Both use base forms after 'didn't'.
items.ed: didn't have, didn't do. Beide verwenden die Grundform nach 'didn't'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.h: After 'didn't', the verb goes back to its base form.
items.hd: Nach 'didn't' geht das Verb zurueck in die Grundform.
items.e: didn't + do (base form). Not 'didn't did' — that's double past marking.
items.ed: didn't + do (Grundform). Nicht 'didn't did' — das ist doppelte Vergangenheitsmarkierung.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He didn't went to the party.
items.c: He didn't go to the party.
a: He didn't go to the party.
He did not go to the party.
items.h: After 'didn't', use the BASE form, not the past form!
items.hd: Nach 'didn't' die GRUNDFORM verwenden, nicht die Vergangenheitsform!
items.e: didn't + go (not went!). 'Didn't' already contains the past — no need for 'went'.
items.ed: didn't + go (nicht went!). 'Didn't' enthaelt bereits die Vergangenheit — 'went' ist nicht nötig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She didn't liked the food.
a: She did not like the food.
items.h: After 'didn't', do we need -ed on the verb?
items.hd: Brauchen wir nach 'didn't' -ed am Verb?
items.e: didn't + like (base form, no -ed). 'Didn't liked' = double past marking.
items.ed: didn't + like (Grundform, kein -ed). 'Didn't liked' = doppelte Vergangenheitsmarkierung.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I no went to school yesterday.
items.c: I didn't go to school yesterday.
a: I didn't go to school yesterday.
I did not go to school yesterday.
items.h: English uses 'didn't + base verb' for past negative, not 'no + past verb'.
items.hd: Englisch verwendet 'didn't + Grundform' für die verneinte Vergangenheit, nicht 'no + Vergangenheitsform'.
items.e: Past negative = didn't + base verb. 'I no went' is an L1 pattern — English needs 'didn't go'.
items.ed: Verneinte Vergangenheit = didn't + Grundform. 'I no went' ist ein L1-Muster — Englisch braucht 'didn't go'.

items.p: You were sick yesterday and stayed home. Make it negative: I went to school. → I _____ to school.
a: I did not go to school.
items.h: went → didn't + go (base form).
items.hd: went → didn't + go (Grundform).
items.e: went (past affirmative) → didn't go (past negative). The base form returns after 'didn't'.
items.ed: went (bejahte Vergangenheit) → didn't go (verneinte Vergangenheit). Die Grundform kommt nach 'didn't' zurueck.

items.p: Your sister was busy with homework last night. Make it negative: She watched TV. → She _____ TV.
items.h: watched → didn't + watch (base form, no -ed!).
items.hd: watched → didn't + watch (Grundform, kein -ed!).
items.e: watched → didn't watch. Remove the -ed and add 'didn't' before the base form.
items.ed: watched → didn't watch. Das -ed entfernen und 'didn't' vor die Grundform setzen.

items.p: Translate into English: Ich habe den Film nicht gesehen.
a: I did not see the film.
I did not see the movie.
items.h: 'nicht gesehen' = didn't see. Use base form after 'didn't'!
items.hd: 'nicht gesehen' = didn't see. Grundform nach 'didn't' verwenden!
items.e: German Perfekt ('habe gesehen') = English past simple ('saw'). Negative: 'didn't see'.
items.ed: Deutsch Perfekt ('habe gesehen') = Englisch Past Simple ('saw'). Verneinung: 'didn't see'.

items.p: We ___ (not/have) any money, so we couldn't buy anything.
items.h: Both negatives use 'didn't' + base form.
items.hd: Beide Verneinungen verwenden 'didn't' + Grundform.
items.e: didn't have, didn't buy. Both irregular verbs return to base form after 'didn't'.
items.ed: didn't have, didn't buy. Beide unregelmäßigen Verben gehen nach 'didn't' in die Grundform zurueck.

items.p: He ___ (not/eat) all the pizza. There was some left.
items.h: ate → didn't + eat (base form).
items.hd: ate → didn't + eat (Grundform).
items.e: ate (irregular past) → didn't eat (negative with base form).
items.ed: ate (unregelmäßige Vergangenheit) → didn't eat (Verneinung mit Grundform).

items.p: Yesterday she _____ (go) to the park.
items.h: go → went. This is an irregular verb — learn it by heart!
items.hd: go → went. Das ist ein unregelmäßiges Verb — lerne es auswendig!
items.e: go → went (irregular). Don't add -ed to irregular verbs!
items.ed: go → went (unregelmäßig). Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben!

items.p: I _____ (see) a great film last night.
items.h: see → saw. Irregular past tense.
items.hd: see → saw. unregelmäßige Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: see → saw (not 'seed'!). This is one of the most common irregular verbs.
items.ed: see → saw (nicht 'seed'!). Das ist eines der häufigsten unregelmäßigen Verben.

items.p: She _____ (write) a letter to her friend.
items.h: write → wrote. Irregular past tense.
items.hd: write → wrote. unregelmäßige Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: write → wrote (not 'writed'). An irregular verb.
items.ed: write → wrote (nicht 'writed'). Ein unregelmäßiges Verb.

items.p: He _____ (think) it was a great idea.
items.h: think → thought. A common irregular verb.
items.hd: think → thought. Ein häufiges unregelmäßiges Verb.
items.e: think → thought (not 'thinked'!). Irregular verbs must be memorised.
items.ed: think → thought (nicht 'thinked'!). unregelmäßige Verben muss man auswendig lernen.

items.p: We _____ (have) pizza for dinner last night.
items.h: have → had. Irregular verb.
items.hd: have → had. unregelmäßiges Verb.
items.e: have → had (not 'haved'!). Simple and common irregular form.
items.ed: have → had (nicht 'haved'!). Einfache und häufige unregelmäßige Form.

items.p: Yesterday I _____ (take) the bus and _____ (come) to school early.
items.h: take → took, come → came. Both irregular!
items.hd: take → took, come → came. Beide unregelmäßig!
items.e: take → took, come → came. Neither takes -ed. Both are irregular.
items.ed: take → took, come → came. Keines bekommt -ed. Beide sind unregelmäßig.

items.p: Which past tense form is correct? run → _____
items.h: run is irregular. Its past form changes the vowel.
items.hd: run ist unregelmäßig. Die Vergangenheitsform ändert den Vokal.
items.e: run → ran (not 'runned'). The vowel changes: u → a.
items.ed: run → ran (nicht 'runned'). Der Vokal ändert sich: u → a.

items.p: Which past form is WRONG?
items.h: One of these forms doesn't exist. Which verb is someone trying to make regular?
items.hd: Eine dieser Formen existiert nicht. Welches Verb versucht jemand regelmäßig zu machen?
items.e: 'Goed' is wrong — 'go' is irregular: go → went. You can't add -ed!
items.ed: 'Goed' ist falsch — 'go' ist unregelmäßig: go → went. Man kann kein -ed anhängen!

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
ds: She makes a beautiful cake yesterday.
items.h: make → made. This verb is irregular.
items.hd: make → made. Dieses Verb ist unregelmäßig.
items.e: make → made (not 'maked'). Irregular verbs don't take -ed.
items.ed: make → made (nicht 'maked'). unregelmäßige Verben bekommen kein -ed.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I catched the ball in the game.
items.c: I caught the ball in the game.
a: I caught the ball in the game.
items.h: catch → caught (not 'catched'!). Irregular verb.
items.hd: catch → caught (nicht 'catched'!). unregelmäßiges Verb.
items.e: catch → caught. Don't add -ed to irregular verbs.
items.ed: catch → caught. Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He thinked about it all day.
items.c: He thought about it all day.
a: He thought about it all day.
items.h: think → thought (not 'thinked'!).
items.hd: think → thought (nicht 'thinked'!).
items.e: think → thought. 'Thinked' doesn't exist — 'think' is irregular.
items.ed: think → thought. 'Thinked' gibt es nicht — 'think' ist unregelmäßig.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She writed a story about her dog.
items.c: She wrote a story about her dog.
a: She wrote a story about her dog.
items.h: write → wrote (not 'writed'!).
items.hd: write → wrote (nicht 'writed'!).
items.e: write → wrote. 'Writed' is a common overgeneralisation error.
items.ed: write → wrote. 'Writed' ist ein häufiger Übergeneralisierungsfehler.

items.p: You're talking about your birthday dinner yesterday. The cake was delicious! Write the past simple: eat → _____
items.h: eat → ate. The vowel changes completely.
items.hd: eat → ate. Der Vokal ändert sich komplett.
items.e: eat → ate (not 'eated'). Irregular verb.
items.ed: eat → ate (nicht 'eated'). unregelmäßiges Verb.

items.p: Your friend got a present from his grandma. She is very generous! Write the past simple: give → _____
items.h: give → gave. The vowel changes: i → a.
items.hd: give → gave. Der Vokal ändert sich: i → a.
items.e: give → gave (not 'gived'). Irregular verb.
items.ed: give → gave (nicht 'gived'). unregelmäßiges Verb.

items.p: Translate into English: Ich bin gestern in die Schule gegangen.
items.h: 'gegangen' = went (past of 'go'). Use past simple, not 'have gone'.
items.hd: 'gegangen' = went (Vergangenheit von 'go'). Verwende Past Simple, nicht 'have gone'.
items.e: German Perfekt 'bin gegangen' = English past simple 'went'. Not 'have gone'!
items.ed: Deutsch Perfekt 'bin gegangen' = Englisch Past Simple 'went'. Nicht 'have gone'!

items.p: Translate into English: Sie hat einen Brief geschrieben und ihn abgeschickt.
items.c: She wrote a letter and sent it.
a: She wrote a letter and sent it.
She wrote a letter and she sent it.
items.h: 'geschrieben' = wrote. 'abgeschickt' = sent. Both irregular!
items.hd: 'geschrieben' = wrote. 'abgeschickt' = sent. Beide unregelmäßig!
items.e: write → wrote, send → sent. German Perfekt = English past simple.
items.ed: write → wrote, send → sent. Deutsch Perfekt = Englisch Past Simple.

items.p: Match the base form with its past simple form:
ds: a: took|b: made|c: came|d: went|e: saw
items.h: Think of each pair: go→?, see→?, take→?, make→?, come→?
items.hd: Denke an jedes Paar: go→?, see→?, take→?, make→?, come→?
items.e: go→went, see→saw, take→took, make→made, come→came.
items.ed: go→went, see→saw, take→took, make→made, come→came.

items.p: Yesterday she ___ (find) a cat in the garden.
items.h: find → found. give → gave. Both irregular!
items.hd: find → found. give → gave. Beide unregelmäßig!
items.e: find → found, give → gave. Both irregular verbs.
items.ed: find → found, give → gave. Beide unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: He ___ (read) the book and then put it on the shelf.
items.h: read → read (same spelling, different pronunciation!). put → put (no change!). say → said.
items.hd: read → read (gleiche Schreibweise, andere Aussprache!). put → put (keine Änderung!). say → said.
items.e: read → read (pronunciation changes: /riːd/ → /red/), put → put (no change), say → said.
items.ed: read → read (Aussprache ändert sich: /riːd/ → /red/), put → put (keine Änderung), say → said.

items.p: I _____ (be going to / visit) my grandma tomorrow.
items.h: Formula: am/is/are + going to + BASE verb.
items.hd: Formel: am/is/are + going to + GRUNDFORM des Verbs.
items.e: I AM going to visit. Don't forget 'am'! And the verb after 'going to' stays in base form.
items.ed: I AM going to visit. Vergiss 'am' nicht! Und das Verb nach 'going to' bleibt in der Grundform.

items.p: She _____ (be going to / fly) to London next week.
items.h: 'She' goes with 'is'. After 'going to', use the base form.
items.hd: 'She' geht mit 'is'. Nach 'going to' verwende die Grundform.
items.e: She IS going to fly. 'She' + 'is' + going to + base verb.
items.ed: She IS going to fly. 'She' + 'is' + going to + Grundform.

items.p: We _____ (be going to / play) football on the beach.
items.h: 'We' + 'are'. After 'going to', use the base form of the verb.
items.hd: 'We' + 'are'. Nach 'going to' verwende die Grundform des Verbs.
items.e: We ARE going to play. 'We' + 'are' + going to + base verb.
items.ed: We ARE going to play. 'We' + 'are' + going to + Grundform.

items.p: He _____ (not / be going to / come) to the party.
items.h: Negative: isn't / aren't + going to + base verb.
items.hd: Verneinung: isn't / aren't + going to + Grundform.
items.e: He isn't going to come. Negative: subject + be + not + going to + base verb.
items.ed: He isn't going to come. Verneinung: Subjekt + be + not + going to + Grundform.

items.p: _____ you _____ (be going to / help) me with my homework?
items.h: Questions: Am/Is/Are + subject + going to + base verb?
items.hd: Fragen: Am/Is/Are + Subjekt + going to + Grundform?
items.e: Are you going to help? Questions invert 'be' and subject.
items.ed: Are you going to help? Fragen tauschen 'be' und Subjekt.

items.p: What _____ you _____ (be going to / do) this weekend?
items.h: Wh-question: What + are/is + subject + going to + base verb?
items.hd: W-Frage: What + are/is + Subjekt + going to + Grundform?
items.e: What are you going to do? Wh-word + be + subject + going to + base verb.
items.ed: What are you going to do? W-Wort + be + Subjekt + going to + Grundform.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: They are going to have a party.
a: They are going to have a party.
ds: They going to have a party.
They are going have a party.
They are going to having a party.
items.h: Check: subject + be + going to + BASE verb.
items.hd: Pruefe: Subjekt + be + going to + GRUNDFORM.
items.e: They ARE going TO have. All three parts needed: be + going to + base verb.
items.ed: They ARE going TO have. Alle drei Teile nötig: be + going to + Grundform.

items.p: Choose the correct question:
items.c: Is she going to visit London?
a: Is she going to visit London?
ds: Does she going to visit London?
She is going to visit London?
Is she going to visiting London?
items.h: Questions: put 'is/are' before the subject.
items.hd: Fragen: stelle 'is/are' vor das Subjekt.
items.e: Is she going to visit? Questions invert 'be' and subject. No 'does'!
items.ed: Is she going to visit? Fragen tauschen 'be' und Subjekt. Kein 'does'!

items.p: Which negative sentence is correct?
items.c: I'm not going to stay at home.
a: I'm not going to stay at home.
ds: I doesn't going to stay at home.
I not going to stay at home.
I'm not going to staying at home.
items.h: To make 'going to' negative, add 'not' after the form of 'be'.
items.hd: Um 'going to' zu verneinen, füge 'not' nach der Form von 'be' ein.
items.e: I'm not going to stay. Negative: be + not + going to + base verb.
items.ed: I'm not going to stay. Verneinung: be + not + going to + Grundform.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I going to play football tomorrow.
items.c: I am going to play football tomorrow.
a: I am going to play football tomorrow.
I'm going to play football tomorrow.
items.h: Something is missing between 'I' and 'going'. What do you need?
items.hd: Zwischen 'I' und 'going' fehlt etwas. Was brauchst du?
items.e: I AM going to play. Don't forget the 'be' auxiliary (am/is/are)!
items.ed: I AM going to play. Vergiss das Hilfsverb 'be' (am/is/are) nicht!

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Do you going to swim?
items.h: 'Going to' questions use 'am/is/are', not 'do/does'.
items.hd: 'Going to' Fragen verwenden 'am/is/are', nicht 'do/does'.
items.e: Are you going to swim? (not 'Do you going to'). Use 'be' for 'going to' questions.
items.ed: Are you going to swim? (nicht 'Do you going to'). Verwende 'be' für 'going to' Fragen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She are going to read a book.
items.c: She is going to read a book.
a: She is going to read a book.
She's going to read a book.
items.h: 'She' is singular — which form of 'be'?
items.hd: 'She' ist Einzahl — welche Form von 'be'?
items.e: She IS going to read. 'She' takes 'is', not 'are'.
items.ed: She IS going to read. 'She' nimmt 'is', nicht 'are'.

items.p: Make it negative: They are going to have a party. → They _____ a party.
a: They aren't going to have a party.
They aren't going to have a party
They are not going to have a party.
they'ren't going to have a party.
items.h: Add 'not' after 'are'. The rest stays the same.
items.hd: Fuege 'not' nach 'are' ein. Der Rest bleibt gleich.
items.e: They aren't going to have. Negative: add 'not' after 'be'.
items.ed: They aren't going to have. Verneinung: 'not' nach 'be' einfuegen.

items.p: Make it a question: He is going to travel to Spain. → _____ he _____ to Spain?
a: Is, going to travel he Is, going to travel to Spain?
Is, going to travel he Is, going to travel to Spain
Is, going to travel he Is, going to travel to Spain
Is, going to travel he's, going to travel to Spain?
items.h: Put 'is' before 'he' to form a question.
items.hd: Stelle 'is' vor 'he', um eine Frage zu bilden.
items.e: Is he going to travel? Invert 'be' and subject for questions.
items.ed: Is he going to travel? 'Be' und Subjekt tauschen für Fragen.

items.p: Translate into English: Ich werde morgen nach Wien fahren.
items.c: I'm going to go to Vienna tomorrow.
a: I'm going to go to Vienna tomorrow.
I am going to go to Vienna tomorrow.
I'm going to travel to Vienna tomorrow.
Tomorrow I'm going to go to Vienna.
I am going to travel to Vienna tomorrow.
Tomorrow I am going to go to Vienna.
Tomorrow i'm going to travel to vienna
Tomorrow i am going to travel to vienna
items.h: 'werde ... fahren' = am going to go/travel. 'morgen' = tomorrow.
items.hd: 'werde ... fahren' = am going to go/travel. 'morgen' = tomorrow.
items.e: German future 'werde fahren' = English 'am going to go'. Plans use 'going to'.
items.ed: Deutsch Futur 'werde fahren' = Englisch 'am going to go'. Plaene verwenden 'going to'.

items.p: Translate into English: Werden sie am Strand schwimmen? – Ja.
items.c: Are they going to swim at the beach? – Yes, they are.
a: Are they going to swim at the beach? – Yes, they are.
Are they going to swim at the beach? - Yes, they are.
Are they going to swim at the beach? Yes, they are.
Are they going to swim at the beach? – Yes, they are
Are they going to swim at the beach? – Yes, they're.
Are they going to swim at the beach? - Yes, they're.
Are they going to swim at the beach? Yes, they're.
items.h: 'Werden sie ...?' = Are they going to ...? Short answer: Yes, they are.
items.hd: 'Werden sie ...?' = Are they going to ...? Kurzantwort: Yes, they are.
items.e: Are they going to swim? Yes, they are. German 'Werden' future = English 'going to'.
items.ed: Are they going to swim? Yes, they are. Deutsch 'Werden'-Futur = Englisch 'going to'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: going / to / she / is / a / read / book
items.c: She is going to read a book.
a: She is going to read a book.
She's going to read a book.
items.h: Subject + be + going to + base verb + object.
items.hd: Subjekt + be + going to + Grundform + Objekt.
items.e: She (subject) + is (be) + going to + read (base verb) + a book (object).
items.ed: She (Subjekt) + is (be) + going to + read (Grundform) + a book (Objekt).

items.p: She is going to visit London. → Ask a yes/no question.
items.c: Is she going to visit London?
a: Is she going to visit London?
items.h: Put 'is' before 'she' to make a question.
items.hd: Stelle 'is' vor 'she', um eine Frage zu bilden.
items.e: Is she going to visit London? Invert subject and 'be' for questions.
items.ed: Is she going to visit London? Tausche Subjekt und 'be' für Fragen.

items.p: ___ they going to play tennis tomorrow?
items.h: Question: Are + they + going to + base verb? Short answer: No, they aren't.
items.hd: Frage: Are + they + going to + Grundform? Kurzantwort: No, they aren't.
items.e: Are they going to play? No, they aren't. Questions and short answers with 'going to'.
items.ed: Are they going to play? No, they aren't. Fragen und Kurzantworten mit 'going to'.

items.p: I ___ (not / be going to / eat) pizza tonight. I'm going to have pasta.
items.h: First = negative (I'm not going to ...). Second = affirmative (I'm going to ...).
items.hd: Erstes = Verneinung (I'm not going to ...). Zweites = Bejahung (I'm going to ...).
items.e: I'm not going to eat pizza. I'm going to have pasta. Negative vs affirmative 'going to'.
items.ed: I'm not going to eat pizza. I'm going to have pasta. Verneinung vs Bejahung mit 'going to'.

items.p: The dentist asks you to open wide. He's checking your... Write the plural: tooth → ___
items.h: Tooth is an irregular plural.
items.hd: Tooth hat eine unregelmäßige Mehrzahl.
items.e: tooth → teeth (irregular plural, learn by heart).
items.ed: tooth → teeth (unregelmäßige Mehrzahl, auswendig lernen).

items.p: ___ your books at page 12, please.
items.h: Commands use the base form of the verb — no subject!
items.hd: Befehle verwenden die Grundform des Verbs — kein Subjekt!
items.e: Imperatives use the base verb only: Open your books. No 'you' or 'to' needed.
items.ed: Imperative verwenden nur die Grundform: Open your books. Kein 'you' oder 'to' nötig.

items.p: A student is eating in class. The teacher is not happy. Make it a negative command: Eat in class. → ___ in class.
items.h: Negative commands: Don't + base verb.
items.hd: Verneinte Befehle: Don't + Grundform.
items.e: Negative imperative: Don't + base verb. Don't eat in class!
items.ed: Verneinender Imperativ: Don't + Grundform. Don't eat in class!

items.p: You're writing a quick text to your friend. Use the short form. Write the short form: I am happy. → ___ happy.
items.h: I am = I'm
items.hd: I am = I'm
items.e: I am → I'm. The apostrophe replaces the missing letter 'a'.
items.ed: I am → I'm. Der Apostroph ersetzt den fehlenden Buchstaben 'a'.

items.p: My sister ___ (be) twelve years old.
items.h: My sister = she → which form of be?
items.hd: My sister = she → welche Form von be?
items.e: My sister = 3rd person singular → is.
items.ed: My sister = 3. Person Einzahl → is.

items.p: You want to check if your friends are from Vienna. Make it a question: They are from Vienna. → ___ from Vienna?
items.h: For questions with be, put the verb before the subject.
items.hd: Für Fragen mit be kommt das Verb vor das Subjekt.
items.e: They are → Are they? Invert subject and verb for questions.
items.ed: They are → Are they? Subjekt und Verb tauschen für Fragen.

items.p: The ball is ___ the box. You can't see it.
items.h: You can see its tail sticking out from the back of the sofa.
items.hd: Du kannst den Schwanz hinten am Sofa herausstehen sehen.
items.e: Behind = at the back of. The cat is behind the sofa.
items.ed: Behind = hinter. Die Katze ist hinter dem Sofa.

items.p: ___ 25 students in our class.
items.h: 25 students = plural → there is or there are?
items.hd: 25 Schüler = Mehrzahl → there is oder there are?
items.e: 25 students is plural → There are.
items.ed: 25 Schüler ist Mehrzahl → There are.

items.p: ___ a new teacher at our school this year.
items.h: A new teacher = one person → singular.
items.hd: Ein neuer Lehrer = eine Person → Einzahl.
items.e: One teacher = singular → There is a new teacher.
items.ed: Ein Lehrer = Einzahl → There is a new teacher.

items.p: Tom and I are friends. ___ go to the same school.
items.h: Brother = male. Which subject pronoun?
items.hd: Brother = männlich. Welches Subjektpronomen?
items.e: Brother = male → He. Subject pronouns replace the noun as subject.
items.ed: Brother = männlich → He. Subjektpronomen ersetzen das Nomen als Subjekt.

items.p: My sister likes sport. ___ plays tennis every day.
items.h: Lisa and Tom = two other people → which pronoun?
items.hd: Lisa und Tom = zwei andere Personen → welches Pronomen?
items.e: Lisa and Tom = plural, not the speaker → They.
items.ed: Lisa und Tom = Mehrzahl, nicht der Sprecher → They.

items.p: She ___ got a beautiful garden.
items.h: She = 3rd person → have got or has got?
items.hd: She = 3. Person → have got oder has got?
items.e: She = 3rd person singular → has got.
items.ed: She = 3. Person Einzahl → has got.

items.p: You want to ask your new neighbour about pets. Make it a question: You have got a pet. → ___ a pet?
items.h: For questions: Have/Has + subject + got?
items.hd: Für Fragen: Have/Has + Subjekt + got?
items.e: You have got → Have you got? Invert have and subject.
items.ed: You have got → Have you got? Have und Subjekt tauschen.

items.p: You see a nice bike in the playground. It belongs to Tom. Rewrite: The bike belongs to Tom. → It is ___ bike.
items.h: To show possession, add 's to the owner's name.
items.hd: Um Besitz zu zeigen, füge 's an den Namen des Besitzers.
items.e: The bike belongs to Tom → Tom's bike. Add 's for possession.
items.ed: Das Fahrrad gehört Tom → Tom's bike. 's zeigt Besitz.

items.p: That is my ___ car. (dad)
items.h: Possessive 's shows that something belongs to someone.
items.hd: Possessives 's zeigt, dass etwas jemandem gehört.
items.e: Lisa's bag = the bag belongs to Lisa. In the other sentences, 's = is.
items.ed: Lisa's bag = die Tasche gehört Lisa. In den anderen Sätzen bedeutet 's = is.

items.p: The film ___ (be) really boring.
items.h: The film = it = 3rd person singular.
items.hd: The film = es = 3. Person Einzahl.
items.e: The film = singular → is. Adjective comes after be: is boring.
items.ed: The film = Einzahl → is. Das Adjektiv kommt nach be: is boring.

items.p: The children ___ (be) very happy today.
items.h: I + which form of be?
items.hd: I + welche Form von be?
items.e: I am tired. I always goes with am.
items.ed: I am tired. I geht immer mit am.

items.p: The day after Monday is ___.
items.h: Days of the week always use the same preposition.
items.hd: Wochentage verwenden immer dieselbe Präposition.
items.e: Days → on. We have PE on Tuesdays.
items.ed: Tage → on. We have PE on Tuesdays.

items.p: The day before Saturday is ___.
items.h: Which preposition goes with days?
items.hd: Welche Präposition steht bei Tagen?
items.e: Days of the week → on. I play football on Saturdays.
items.ed: Wochentage → on. I play football on Saturdays.

items.p: Lisa and Tom love ___ school. It's really great!
items.h: Tom = he → which possessive adjective?
items.hd: Tom = er → welcher Possessivbegleiter?
items.e: Tom = he → his. His new bike.
items.ed: Tom = er → his. His new bike.

items.p: Your friend says she can swim, but you can't. Make it negative: I can swim. → I ___ swim.
items.h: The negative of can is...?
items.hd: Die Verneinung von can ist...?
items.e: can → can't (or cannot). I can't swim.
items.ed: can → can't (oder cannot). I can't swim.

items.p: Make it a question: She can play the guitar. → ___ the guitar?
items.h: For can questions, put can before the subject.
items.hd: Für can-Fragen kommt can vor das Subjekt.
items.e: She can → Can she? No do-support needed with can.
items.ed: She can → Can she? Kein do/does bei can-Fragen.

items.p: She ___ (walk) to school every morning.
items.h: She = 3rd person → add -s to the verb.
items.hd: She = 3. Person → -s an das Verb hängen.
items.e: She walks. 3rd person singular adds -s: walk → walks.
items.ed: She walks. 3. Person Einzahl bekommt -s: walk → walks.

items.p: He ___ (watch) TV after dinner every day.
items.h: Verbs ending in -ch add -es in 3rd person.
items.hd: Verben auf -ch bekommen -es in der 3. Person.
items.e: watch → watches. Verbs ending in -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -o add -es.
items.ed: watch → watches. Verben auf -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -o bekommen -es.

items.p: We have got ___ friends at school.
items.h: a lot ___ friends — which little word is missing?
items.hd: a lot ___ friends — welches kleine Wort fehlt?
items.e: a lot of = lots of = many. Always: a lot OF + noun.
items.ed: a lot of = lots of = viele. Immer: a lot OF + Nomen.

items.p: There is ___ water in the bottle.
items.h: Something is missing before 'lot'.
items.hd: Vor 'lot' fehlt etwas.
items.e: a lot of — don't forget the 'a'! There are A lot of books.
items.ed: a lot of — vergiss das 'a' nicht! There are A lot of books.

items.p: Your sister says she likes pizza, but you're different. Make it negative: She likes pizza. → She ___ pizza.
items.h: She = 3rd person → doesn't + base verb.
items.hd: She = 3. Person → doesn't + Grundform.
items.e: She likes → She doesn't like. 3rd person: doesn't + base verb (no -s!).
items.ed: She likes → She doesn't like. 3. Person: doesn't + Grundform (kein -s!).

items.p: Everyone thinks you like spiders, but you don't. Make it negative: I like spiders. → I ___ spiders.
items.h: I/you/we/they → don't + base verb.
items.hd: I/you/we/they → don't + Grundform.
items.e: I like → I don't like. Use don't (not doesn't) for I/you/we/they.
items.ed: I like → I don't like. Verwende don't (nicht doesn't) für I/you/we/they.

items.p: I want ___ apple from the fruit bowl.
items.h: Astronaut starts with a vowel sound (a-). Use a or an?
items.hd: Astronaut beginnt mit einem Vokal (a-). a oder an?
items.e: Before vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u) use AN: an astronaut.
items.ed: Vor Vokalen (a, e, i, o, u) verwende AN: an astronaut.

items.p: She is ___ best student in the class.
items.h: Musical instruments use a special article: play ___ piano.
items.hd: Musikinstrumente verwenden einen bestimmten Artikel: play ___ piano.
items.e: Musical instruments use the: play the violin, play the piano.
items.ed: Musikinstrumente verwenden the: play the violin, play the piano.

items.p: She ___ does her homework on time. (100% of the time)
items.h: Every single day = 100% of the time.
items.hd: Jeden einzelnen Tag = 100% der Zeit.
items.e: Every single day = always (100%). I always eat breakfast.
items.ed: Jeden einzelnen Tag = always (100%). I always eat breakfast.

items.p: He is ___ late for school. (0% of the time)
items.h: The teacher is not happy — so it happens a lot.
items.hd: Die Lehrerin ist nicht glücklich — es passiert also häufig.
items.e: The teacher is unhappy → he is often/usually/always late.
items.ed: Die Lehrerin ist unglücklich → er ist often/usually/always zu spät.

items.p: You want to ask your new classmate about football. Make it a question: You like football. → ___ football?
items.h: For I/you/we/they questions: Do + subject + base verb?
items.hd: Für I/you/we/they Fragen: Do + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: You like → Do you like? Use do for I/you/we/they questions.
items.ed: You like → Do you like? Verwende do für I/you/we/they Fragen.

items.p: You think your friend plays tennis. Ask to be sure. Make it a question: He plays tennis. → ___ tennis?
items.h: He = 3rd person → Does + subject + base verb?
items.hd: He = 3. Person → Does + Subjekt + Grundform?
items.e: He plays → Does he play? 3rd person uses does (and verb goes back to base form!).
items.ed: He plays → Does he play? 3. Person verwendet does (und das Verb geht zurück in die Grundform!).

items.p: I like my teacher. I listen to ___ every day.
items.h: 'my teacher' comes after 'like' → we need an OBJECT pronoun.
items.hd: 'my teacher' kommt nach 'like' → wir brauchen ein OBJEKT-Pronomen.
items.e: After verbs (like, help, see), use object pronouns: her/him, not she/he.
items.ed: Nach Verben (like, help, see) verwende Objektpronomen: her/him, nicht she/he.

items.p: Tom and Lisa are my friends. I play with ___ every day.
items.h: Tom and Lisa = they (subject) → what's the object form?
items.hd: Tom und Lisa = they (Subjekt) → was ist die Objektform?
items.e: they (subject) → them (object). Can you see them?
items.ed: they (Subjekt) → them (Objekt). Can you see them?

items.p: ___ is your favourite colour?
items.h: The answer is 'Maths' — a thing, not a person or place.
items.hd: Die Antwort ist 'Maths' — eine Sache, keine Person oder ein Ort.
items.e: What asks about things. What is your favourite subject?
items.ed: What fragt nach Dingen. What is your favourite subject?

items.p: I brush my teeth ___ a day. (3x)
items.h: Morning and evening = two times.
items.hd: Morgens und abends = zweimal.
items.e: Morning + evening = twice (= two times) a day.
items.ed: Morgens + abends = twice (= zweimal) am Tag.

items.p: I like cats ___ my brother likes dogs.
items.h: She was hungry, then she ate. Two actions that go together.
items.hd: Sie war hungrig, dann hat sie gegessen. Zwei zusammengehörende Handlungen.
items.e: And connects two related actions: hungry → ate.
items.ed: And verbindet zusammenhängende Handlungen: hungrig → gegessen.

items.p: Your friend does sport every Saturday. Which sentence describes her habit correctly?
ds: She is playing tennis every Saturday.
items.h: Habits use present simple. Don't forget the -s for he/she/it!
items.hd: Gewohnheiten brauchen Present Simple. Vergiss das -s für he/she/it nicht!
items.e: 'Every Saturday' signals a regular habit → present simple. She plays (3rd person -s).
items.ed: 'Every Saturday' zeigt eine regelmäßige Gewohnheit → Present Simple. She plays (3. Person -s).

items.p: You talk about your morning routine. Which sentence is correct?
ds: I am usually walking to school.
items.h: 'Usually' = a routine. Use present simple. 'I' doesn't need -s.
items.hd: 'Usually' = eine Routine. Verwende Present Simple. 'I' braucht kein -s.
items.e: 'Usually' signals a routine → present simple. I walk (no -s for I/you/we/they).
items.ed: 'Usually' zeigt eine Routine → Present Simple. I walk (kein -s für I/you/we/they).

items.p: Your friends are in the park right now. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: They are playing football right now.
a: They are playing football right now.
items.h: 'Right now' = present continuous: am/is/are + verb-ing.
items.hd: 'Right now' = Present Continuous: am/is/are + Verb-ing.
items.e: 'Right now' + are playing = present continuous. They are playing football.
items.ed: 'Right now' + are playing = Present Continuous. They are playing football.

items.p: You see your brother in the kitchen. He has flour on his hands. Which sentence fits?
items.c: He is making a cake at the moment.
a: He is making a cake at the moment.
ds: He makes a cake at the moment.
He made a cake at the moment.
He is make a cake at the moment.
items.h: 'At the moment' = happening now. Use is + verb-ing.
items.hd: 'At the moment' = passiert gerade. Verwende is + Verb-ing.
items.e: 'At the moment' + is making = present continuous for actions happening now.
items.ed: 'At the moment' + is making = Present Continuous für Handlungen, die gerade jetzt passieren.

items.p: Your teacher asks about the school trip last Friday. Which answer is correct?
items.h: It = singular → was (not 'were'). The trip already happened.
items.hd: It = Einzahl → was (nicht 'were'). Der Ausflug ist schon vorbei.
items.e: 'It was' — use 'was' for I/he/she/it in the past of 'be'.
items.ed: 'It was' — verwende 'was' für I/he/she/it in der Vergangenheit von 'be'.

items.p: Your friend tells you about last weekend. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I watched a film with my family last Saturday.
a: I watched a film with my family last Saturday.
ds: I watch a film with my family last Saturday.
I was watched a film with my family last Saturday.
I watchd a film with my family last Saturday.
items.h: Past simple of regular verbs: add -ed. Watch → watched.
items.hd: Past Simple regelmäßiger Verben: hänge -ed an. Watch → watched.
items.e: 'Last Saturday' = past. Regular past simple: watch → watched.
items.ed: 'Last Saturday' = Vergangenheit. Regelmäßiges Past Simple: watch → watched.

items.p: You are telling your teacher about yesterday. Which sentence uses the past correctly?
items.c: I ate lunch at school yesterday.
a: I ate lunch at school yesterday.
ds: I eated lunch at school yesterday.
I eat lunch at school yesterday.
I was eat lunch at school yesterday.
items.h: 'Eat' is irregular. Don't add -ed! Learn the past form by heart.
items.hd: 'Eat' ist unregelmäßig. Hänge kein -ed an! Lerne die Vergangenheitsform auswendig.
items.e: 'Eat' is irregular: eat → ate. Do not add -ed to irregular verbs.
items.ed: 'Eat' ist unregelmäßig: eat → ate. Hänge kein -ed an unregelmäßige Verben.

items.p: Your family has plans for the weekend. Which sentence talks about a future plan correctly?
items.c: We are going to visit grandma on Sunday.
a: We are going to visit grandma on Sunday.
ds: We going to visit grandma on Sunday.
We are go to visit grandma on Sunday.
We are going visit grandma on Sunday.
items.h: Plans: am/is/are + going to + base verb.
items.hd: Pläne: am/is/are + going to + Grundform.
items.e: 'Going to' for plans: We are going to visit. Full form: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb.
items.ed: 'Going to' für Pläne: We are going to visit. Volle Form: Subjekt + am/is/are + going to + Grundform.

items.p: Your friend asks about your holiday plans. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I am going to travel to Italy this summer.
a: I am going to travel to Italy this summer.
ds: I going to travel to Italy this summer.
I am going to travelling to Italy this summer.
I am going travel to Italy this summer.
items.h: I + am + going to + base verb (no -ing on the main verb!).
items.hd: I + am + going to + Grundform (kein -ing am Hauptverb!).
items.e: 'I am going to travel' — after 'going to', always use the base form.
items.ed: 'I am going to travel' — nach 'going to' immer die Grundform verwenden.

items.p: Statement: She was at home. Ask a YES/NO question.
items.h: For be questions, put was/were before the subject.
items.hd: Für be-Fragen kommt was/were vor das Subjekt.
items.e: She was → Was she? Invert was and subject.
items.ed: She was → Was she? Was und Subjekt tauschen.

items.p: Statement: She likes chocolate. Ask about WHAT.
items.h: She likes → Does she like? Then add What at the start.
items.hd: She likes → Does she like? Dann What an den Anfang.
items.e: What does she like? Use does for 3rd person questions, verb back to base form.
items.ed: What does she like? Verwende does für 3. Person Fragen, Verb in Grundform.

items.p: Statement: He went to school. Ask a YES/NO question.
items.h: Past questions use Did + subject + base verb.
items.hd: Fragen in der Vergangenheit: Did + Subjekt + Grundform.
items.e: Did he go? (not 'Did he went?'). Did + base verb, not past form.

items.h: Es gibt = There is/are.
items.hd: There is = Es gibt.
items.e: There is.
items.ed: There is.

items.p: Translate: Gibt es ein Kino?
items.h: Gibt es = Is there.
items.hd: Is there = Gibt es.
items.e: Is there?
items.ed: Gibt es?

items.p: Put in order: is / a / there / in / cat / the / garden
items.c: There is a cat in the garden.
a: There is a cat in the garden.
items.h: There is + noun + place.
items.hd: There is + Nomen + Ort.
items.e: There is a cat in the garden.
items.ed: There is a cat in the garden.

items.p: Put in order: are / any / there / near / shops / here
items.c: Are there any shops near here?
a: Are there any shops near here?
items.h: Are there + noun?
items.hd: Are there + Nomen?
items.e: Are there any shops near here?
items.ed: Are there any shops near here?

items.p: Match: 1) a dog (1) 2) two birds (2+) 3) no milk (0) 4) three books (2+) — a) There are b) There is c) There isn't d) There are
items.h: Singular=is, plural=are, negative=isn't.
items.hd: Einzahl=is, Mehrzahl=are.
items.e: Singular→is, plural→are.
items.ed: Einzahl→is, Mehrzahl→are.

items.p: You arrive at a new school. Which sentence describes what you see?
items.c: There are lots of students in the playground.
a: There are lots of students in the playground.
items.h: Plural → There are.
items.hd: Mehrzahl → There are.
items.e: There are.
items.ed: There are.

items.p: I ___ (not / like) fish.
items.h: I/you/we/they use 'don't'. He/she/it uses 'doesn't'.
items.hd: I/you/we/they verwenden 'don't'. He/she/it verwendet 'doesn't'.
items.e: For I/you/we/they, use don't (do not) + base verb: I don't like.
items.ed: Für I/you/we/they verwende don't (do not) + Grundform: I don't like.

items.p: ___ you like pizza? — Yes, I ___.
items.h: You = not 3rd person, so use 'Do'. Short answer: Yes, I do.
items.hd: You = nicht 3. Person, also verwende 'Do'. Kurzantwort: Yes, I do.
items.e: Do + I/you/we/they + base verb? Short answer: Yes, I do. / No, I don't.
items.ed: Do + I/you/we/they + Grundform? Kurzantwort: Yes, I do. / No, I don't.

items.p: I _____ (not/see) the film yesterday.
items.h: Past negative: didn't + BASE verb (no -ed, no irregular form!).
items.hd: Verneinte Vergangenheit: didn't + GRUNDFORM des Verbs (kein -ed, keine unregelmäßige Form!).
items.e: didn't + base verb: didn't SEE (not 'didn't saw'!). 'Didn't' already shows past tense.
items.ed: didn't + Grundform: didn't SEE (nicht 'didn't saw'!). 'Didn't' zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit an.

items.p: Sort these nouns by their plural rule.
items.h: Most nouns add -s. Nouns ending in s, x, ch, sh add -es. Some are irregular.
items.hd: Die meisten Nomen bekommen -s. Nomen auf s, x, ch, sh bekommen -es. Einige sind unregelmaeßig.
items.e: Plural rules: cat→cats (add -s), bus→buses (add -es), child→children (irregular).
items.ed: Pluralregeln: cat→cats (-s), bus→buses (-es), child→children (unregelmaeßig).

items.p: Sort: which plural rule applies?
items.h: Consonant + y → -ies. Vowel + y → just -s (days, boys).
items.hd: Konsonant + y → -ies. Vokal + y → nur -s (days, boys).
items.e: baby→babies (-y to -ies), glass→glasses (-es), girl→girls (-s).
items.ed: baby→babies (-y zu -ies), glass→glasses (-es), girl→girls (-s).

items.p: Find the pairs: singular ↔ plural.
items.h: These nouns have irregular plural forms.
items.hd: Diese Nomen haben unregelmaeßige Mehrzahlformen.
items.e: Irregular plurals: child→children, mouse→mice, tooth→teeth.
items.ed: Unregelmaeßige Mehrzahl: child→children, mouse→mice, tooth→teeth.

items.p: Plural of "child":
items.h: This is an irregular plural.
items.hd: Das ist eine unregelmaeßige Mehrzahl.
items.e: child → children (irregular plural).
items.ed: child → children (unregelmaeßige Mehrzahl).

items.p: Plural of "woman":
items.h: This is an irregular plural — the vowel changes.
items.hd: Unregelmaeßige Mehrzahl — der Vokal aendert sich.
items.e: woman → women (irregular: vowel change).
items.ed: woman → women (unregelmaeßig: Vokelaenderung).

items.p: Sort: positive or negative command?
items.c: {"Positive":["Sit down.","Open your books.","Listen carefully.","Come here."],"Negative":["Don't run!","Don't speak German.","Don't touch that.","Don't be late."]}
items.h: Positive commands use the base verb. Negative commands start with Don't.
items.hd: Positive: Grundform. Negativ: Don't + Grundform.
items.e: Positive: base verb (Sit down). Negative: Don't + base verb (Don't run).
items.ed: Positiv: Grundform (Sit down). Negativ: Don't + Grundform (Don't run).

items.p: Sort: full form or contraction?
items.c: {"Full Form":["I am|I am → I'm","he is|he is → he's","we are|we are → we're","they have|they have → they've","do not|do not → don't"],"Contraction":["I'm|I'm = I am","he's|he's = he is","we're|we're = we are","they've|they've = they have","don't|don't = do not"]}
items.h: Contractions shorten two words into one using an apostrophe.
items.hd: Kurzformen verkuerzen zwei Woerter zu einem mit Apostroph.
items.e: I am → I'm, he is → he's, do not → don't.
items.ed: I am → I'm, he is → he's, do not → don't.

items.p: Find the pairs: full form ↔ contraction.
items.h: Each full form has a contracted version.
items.hd: Jede Langform hat eine Kurzform.
items.e: I am→I'm, she is→she's, do not→don't, cannot→can't.
items.ed: I am→I'm, she is→she's, do not→don't, cannot→can't.

items.p: Sort: which form of "be" goes with which subject?
items.h: I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.hd: I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.e: The verb "be" changes form: am (I), is (he/she/it), are (you/we/they).
items.ed: Das Verb "be" aendert sich: am (I), is (he/she/it), are (you/we/they).

items.p: Sort: affirmative or negative form of "be"?
items.c: {"Affirmative":["I am","she is","they are","he's"],"Negative":["I'm not","she isn't","they aren't","he's not"]}
items.h: Negative be: am not, isn't, aren't.
items.hd: Negatives be: am not, isn't, aren't.
items.e: Affirmative: I am, she is. Negative: I'm not, she isn't.
items.ed: Bejahend: I am, she is. Verneinend: I'm not, she isn't.

items.p: Find the pairs: subject ↔ correct "be" form.
items.h: I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.hd: I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.e: Subject-verb agreement: I am, he is, they are.
items.ed: Subjekt-Verb-Uebereinstimmung: I am, he is, they are.

items.p: Sort: which preposition — in, on, or under?
items.c: {"in":["my bag|in my bag","the box|in the box","the kitchen|in the kitchen"],"on":["the wall|on the wall","the table|on the table","the floor|on the floor"],"under":["the bed|under the bed","the chair|under the chair","the desk|under the desk"]}
items.h: in = inside, on = on a surface, under = below.
items.hd: in = drinnen, on = auf einer Oberflaeche, under = unter.
items.e: in (inside): in my bag. on (surface): on the wall. under (below): under the bed.
items.ed: in (drinnen): in my bag. on (Oberflaeche): on the wall. under (darunter): under the bed.

items.p: Sort: there is or there are?
items.c: {"there is":["a cat","a museum","one student","a swimming pool"],"there are":["two dogs","twenty students","three books","many shops"]}
items.h: there is = singular. there are = plural.
items.hd: there is = Einzahl. there are = Mehrzahl.
items.e: there is + singular (a cat). there are + plural (two dogs).
items.ed: there is + Einzahl (a cat). there are + Mehrzahl (two dogs).

items.p: Find the pairs: name/noun ↔ subject pronoun.
items.c: [["Tom","he"],["Lisa","she"],["the dog","it"],["Tom and I","we"],["the children","they"],["my friend (talking to)","you"]]
items.h: Subject pronouns replace nouns: he, she, it, we, they, you.
items.hd: Subjektpronomen ersetzen Nomen: he, she, it, we, they, you.
items.e: Tom→he, Lisa→she, the dog→it, the children→they.
items.ed: Tom→he, Lisa→she, the dog→it, the children→they.

items.p: Sort: singular or plural pronoun?
items.h: Singular = one person/thing. Plural = more than one. "You" can be both but is treated as plural.
items.hd: Einzahl = eine Person/Sache. Mehrzahl = mehrere.
items.e: Singular: I, he, she, it. Plural: we, they, you.
items.ed: Einzahl: I, he, she, it. Mehrzahl: we, they, you.

items.p: Sort: "have got" or "has got"?
items.h: I/you/we/they have got. He/she/it has got.
items.hd: I/you/we/they have got. He/she/it has got.
items.e: have got for I/you/we/they. has got for he/she/it.
items.ed: have got fuer I/you/we/they. has got fuer he/she/it.

items.p: Find the pairs: affirmative ↔ negative.
items.c: [["I've got","I haven't got"],["she's got","she hasn't got"],["we've got","we haven't got"],["he's got","he hasn't got"],["they've got","they haven't got"]]
items.h: have got → haven't got. has got → hasn't got.
items.hd: have got → haven't got. has got → hasn't got.
items.e: Affirmative→Negative: I've got→I haven't got, she's got→she hasn't got.
items.ed: Bejahend→Verneinend: I've got→I haven't got, she's got→she hasn't got.

items.p: Find the pairs: which means the same?
items.c: [["Lisa's cat","the cat of Lisa"],["my mum's car","the car of my mum"],["Tom's book","the book of Tom"],["the dog's ball","the ball of the dog"],["Dad's phone","the phone of Dad"]]
items.h: Name's + thing = the thing of (the) name.
items.hd: Name's + Sache = die Sache von Name.
items.e: Lisa's cat = the cat belonging to Lisa.
items.ed: Lisa's cat = die Katze, die Lisa gehoert.

items.p: Sort: adjective or noun?
items.h: Adjectives describe nouns. Nouns are people, places, things.
items.hd: Adjektive beschreiben Nomen. Nomen sind Personen, Orte, Dinge.
items.e: Adjectives: happy, tall, tired. Nouns: dog, school, teacher.
items.ed: Adjektive: happy, tall, tired. Nomen: dog, school, teacher.

items.p: Sort: school day or weekend?
items.h: Monday to Friday = school days. Saturday and Sunday = weekend.
items.hd: Montag bis Freitag = Schultage. Samstag und Sonntag = Wochenende.
items.e: School days: Mon-Fri. Weekend: Sat-Sun. Days always start with a capital letter.
items.ed: Schultage: Mo-Fr. Wochenende: Sa-So. Tage beginnen immer mit Großbuchstaben.

items.p: Spell this day:
items.h: This day has a silent "d" — Wed-NES-day.
items.hd: Dieser Tag hat ein stummes "d" — Wed-NES-day.
items.e: Wednesday — one of the trickiest days to spell!
items.ed: Wednesday — einer der schwierigsten Tage zum Buchstabieren!

items.p: Spell this day:
items.h: Thurs-day, not "Tursday".
items.hd: Thurs-day, nicht "Tursday".
items.e: Thursday — remember the "h" after T.
items.ed: Thursday — denke an das "h" nach dem T.

items.p: Find the pairs: subject pronoun ↔ possessive adjective.
items.h: Each subject pronoun has a matching possessive adjective.
items.hd: Jedes Subjektpronomen hat ein passendes besitzanzeigendes Adjektiv.
items.e: I→my, you→your, he→his, she→her, it→its, we→our.
items.ed: I→my, you→your, he→his, she→her, it→its, we→our.

items.p: Sort: subject pronoun or possessive adjective?
items.h: Subject pronouns do the action. Possessive adjectives show ownership.
items.hd: Subjektpronomen fuehren die Handlung aus. Possessive Adjektive zeigen Besitz.
items.e: Subject: I, he, she. Possessive: my, his, her.
items.ed: Subjekt: I, he, she. Possessiv: my, his, her.

items.p: Sort: can (ability) or can't (no ability)?
items.c: {"can":["swim well","speak English","ride a bike","play guitar"],"can't":["fly","drive a car","speak Chinese","cook"]}
items.h: can = I am able to. can't = I am not able to.
items.hd: can = Ich kann. can't = Ich kann nicht.
items.e: can + base verb for ability. can't + base verb for no ability.
items.ed: can + Grundform fuer Faehigkeit. can't + Grundform fuer keine Faehigkeit.

items.p: Which subjects use the base form, and which add -s?
items.c: {"Base form (no -s)":["I + play|I play","you + play|you play","we + play|we play","they + play|they play"],"Third person (-s)":["he + play|he plays","she + play|she plays","it + play|it plays"]}
items.h: I/you/we/they use the base form. He/she/it adds -s.
items.hd: I/you/we/they Grundform. He/she/it bekommt -s.
items.e: I/you/we/they use the base form (play). He/she/it adds -s (plays).
items.ed: I/you/we/they: Grundform (play). He/she/it bekommt -s (plays).

items.p: How does each verb form the third person -s?
items.h: -s for most verbs. -es for verbs ending in o, ch, sh, s, x. -y→-ies after consonant.
items.hd: -s fuer die meisten. -es fuer Verben auf o, ch, sh, s, x. -y→-ies nach Konsonant.
items.e: play→plays (-s), go→goes (-es), study→studies (-ies).
items.ed: play→plays (-s), go→goes (-es), study→studies (-ies).

items.p: Find the pairs: base form ↔ third person form.
items.h: Most verbs add -s. Some are irregular (have→has, do→does).
items.hd: Die meisten Verben bekommen -s. Einige sind unregelmaeßig.
items.e: play→plays, go→goes, watch→watches, study→studies, have→has.
items.ed: play→plays, go→goes, watch→watches, study→studies, have→has.

items.p: Sort: countable or uncountable noun?
items.h: "A lot of" works with both countable and uncountable nouns.
items.hd: "A lot of" funktioniert mit zaehlbaren und unzaehlbaren Nomen.
items.e: Countable: books, friends. Uncountable: water, homework. "A lot of" works with both.
items.ed: Zaehlbar: books, friends. Unzaehlbar: water, homework. "A lot of" passt fuer beides.

items.p: Sort: "don't" or "doesn't"?
items.c: {"don't":["I ___ like fish.|I don't like fish.","We ___ have homework.|We don't have homework.","They ___ play tennis.|They don't play tennis."],"doesn't":["She ___ eat meat.|She doesn't eat meat.","He ___ like maths.|He doesn't like maths.","It ___ work.|It doesn't work."]}
items.h: I/you/we/they + don't. He/she/it + doesn't.
items.hd: I/you/we/they + don't. He/she/it + doesn't.
items.e: don't for I/you/we/they. doesn't for he/she/it. Base verb follows (no -s!).
items.ed: don't fuer I/you/we/they. doesn't fuer he/she/it. Grundform folgt (kein -s!).

items.p: Sort: does it need "a" or "an"?
items.c: {"a":["dog|a dog","cat|a cat","school|a school","big house|a big house","university|a university"],"an":["apple|an apple","egg|an egg","ice cream|an ice cream","orange|an orange","hour|an hour"]}
items.h: "a" before consonant sounds. "an" before vowel sounds. Watch out: "hour" starts with a vowel sound, "university" starts with a consonant sound!
items.hd: "a" vor Konsonantenlaut. "an" vor Vokallaut. Achtung: "hour" beginnt mit Vokallaut, "university" mit Konsonantenlaut!
items.e: a dog, a university (consonant sound). an apple, an hour (vowel sound).
items.ed: a dog, a university (Konsonantenlaut). an apple, an hour (Vokallaut).

items.p: Sort by meaning: how often?
items.h: always=100%, usually=80%, sometimes=50%, never=0%.
items.hd: always=100%, usually=80%, sometimes=50%, never=0%.
items.e: Frequency scale: always > usually > often > sometimes > never.
items.ed: Haeufigkeitsskala: always > usually > often > sometimes > never.

items.p: Sort: does the question start with "Do" or "Does"?
items.c: {"Do":["___ you like pizza?|Do you like pizza?","___ they play football?|Do they play football?","___ we have homework?|Do we have homework?","___ I need a pen?|Do I need a pen?"],"Does":["___ she swim?|Does she swim?","___ he eat meat?|Does he eat meat?","___ it rain a lot?|Does it rain a lot?"]}
items.h: Do + I/you/we/they. Does + he/she/it.
items.hd: Do + I/you/we/they. Does + he/she/it.
items.e: Do you like...? Does she like...? (no -s on the verb after does!).
items.ed: Do you like...? Does she like...? (kein -s am Verb nach does!).

items.p: Find the pairs: subject pronoun ↔ object pronoun.
items.h: Subject does the action. Object receives it.
items.hd: Subjekt fuehrt die Handlung aus. Objekt empfaengt sie.
items.e: Subject→Object: I→me, he→him, she→her, we→us, they→them.
items.ed: Subjekt→Objekt: I→me, he→him, she→her, we→us, they→them.

items.p: Sort: subject pronoun or object pronoun?
items.h: Subject pronouns = before verb. Object pronouns = after verb or preposition.
items.hd: Subjektpronomen = vor Verb. Objektpronomen = nach Verb oder Praeposition.
items.e: Subject: I, he, she. Object: me, him, her.
items.ed: Subjekt: I, he, she. Objekt: me, him, her.

items.p: Find the pairs: question word ↔ what it asks about.
items.c: [["who","a person"],["what","a thing"],["where","a place"],["when","a time"],["why","a reason"],["how","a way/method"]]
items.h: Each question word asks about a different type of information.
items.hd: Jedes Fragewort fragt nach einer anderen Art von Information.
items.e: who=person, what=thing, where=place, when=time, why=reason, how=way.
items.ed: who=Person, what=Sache, where=Ort, when=Zeit, why=Grund, how=Art.

items.p: Sort: which demonstrative — near (here) or far (there)?
items.c: {"this / these (near)":["book (here, one)|this book","shoes (here, many)|these shoes","___ is nice. (here)|This is nice.","___ are mine. (here)|These are mine."],"that / those (far)":["house (there, one)|that house","children (there, many)|those children","___ is big. (there)|That is big.","___ are old. (there)|Those are old."]}
items.h: this/these = near. that/those = far.
items.hd: this/these = nah. that/those = fern.
items.e: Near: this (singular), these (plural). Far: that (singular), those (plural).
items.ed: Nah: this (Einzahl), these (Mehrzahl). Fern: that (Einzahl), those (Mehrzahl).

items.p: Sort: singular or plural demonstrative?
items.h: this/that = one thing. these/those = more than one.
items.hd: this/that = eine Sache. these/those = mehrere.
items.e: Singular: this, that. Plural: these, those.
items.ed: Einzahl: this, that. Mehrzahl: these, those.

items.p: Sort: present simple or present continuous?
items.c: {"Present Simple":["I walk to school.","She eats breakfast.","We play tennis.","He reads books."],"Present Continuous":["I'm walking now.","She's eating lunch.","We're playing now.","He's reading."]}
items.h: Present simple = habits/routines. Present continuous = happening now.
items.hd: Present simple = Gewohnheiten. Present continuous = gerade jetzt.
items.e: Present simple: I walk (every day). Present continuous: I'm walking (now).
items.ed: Present simple: I walk (jeden Tag). Present continuous: I'm walking (jetzt).

items.p: Find the pairs: base form ↔ -ing form.
items.h: -ing spelling: drop -e (make→making), double consonant (run→running).
items.hd: -ing Schreibung: -e weg (make→making), Konsonant verdoppeln (run→running).
items.e: play→playing, make→making (drop e), run→running (double n).
items.ed: play→playing, make→making (-e weg), run→running (n verdoppeln).

items.p: -ing form of "make":
items.h: Drop the final -e before adding -ing.
items.hd: Das finale -e faellt vor -ing weg.
items.e: make → making (drop the -e before -ing).
items.ed: make → making (das -e faellt vor -ing weg).

items.p: -ing form of "swim":
items.h: Short vowel + single consonant → double the consonant before -ing.
items.hd: Kurzer Vokal + einfacher Konsonant → verdoppeln vor -ing.
items.e: swim → swimming (double the m before -ing).
items.ed: swim → swimming (das m wird vor -ing verdoppelt).

items.p: -ing form of "write":
items.h: Drop the final -e before adding -ing.
items.hd: Das finale -e faellt vor -ing weg.
items.e: write → writing (drop the -e before -ing).
items.ed: write → writing (das -e faellt vor -ing weg).

items.p: Sort: "was" or "were"?
items.h: I/he/she/it + was. You/we/they + were.
items.hd: I/he/she/it + was. You/we/they + were.
items.e: was for I/he/she/it. were for you/we/they.
items.ed: was fuer I/he/she/it. were fuer you/we/they.

items.p: Sort: affirmative or negative past of "be"?
items.c: {"Affirmative":["I was happy.","She was at home.","They were late."],"Negative":["I wasn't tired.","She wasn't angry.","They weren't ready."]}
items.h: Negative past of be: wasn't, weren't.
items.hd: Verneintes be in der Vergangenheit: wasn't, weren't.
items.e: Affirmative: was/were. Negative: wasn't/weren't.
items.ed: Bejahend: was/were. Verneinend: wasn't/weren't.

items.p: Find the pairs: cardinal ↔ ordinal number.
items.h: Most ordinals add -th. Exceptions: first, second, third, fifth, twelfth.
items.hd: Die meisten Ordnungszahlen bekommen -th. Ausnahmen: first, second, third, fifth, twelfth.
items.e: one→first, two→second, three→third, five→fifth.
items.ed: one→first, two→second, three→third, five→fifth.

items.p: Ordinal form of "eight":
items.h: Just add -th to "eight" — no spelling change.
items.hd: Einfach -th an "eight" anhaengen — keine Aenderung.
items.e: eight → eighth (add -th).
items.ed: eight → eighth (-th anhaengen).

items.p: Ordinal form of "twelve":
items.h: twelve changes to twelf- before adding -th.
items.hd: twelve aendert sich zu twelf- vor -th.
items.e: twelve → twelfth (ve changes to f before -th).
items.ed: twelve → twelfth (ve aendert sich zu f vor -th).

items.p: Sort: which preposition — at, in, or on?
items.c: {"at":["5 o'clock|at 5 o'clock","night|at night","the weekend|at the weekend","noon|at noon"],"in":["March|in March","summer|in summer","the morning|in the morning","2024|in 2024"],"on":["Monday|on Monday","my birthday|on my birthday","the 5th of May|on the 5th of May","Fridays|on Fridays"]}
items.h: at = clock times/fixed points. in = months/seasons/parts of day. on = days/dates.
items.hd: at = Uhrzeiten. in = Monate/Jahreszeiten/Tageszeiten. on = Tage/Daten.
items.e: at 5 o'clock (time), in March (month), on Monday (day).
items.ed: at 5 o'clock (Uhrzeit), in March (Monat), on Monday (Tag).

items.p: How does each verb form the past tense?
items.h: Most verbs: add -ed. Ends in -e: add -d. Short CVC: double last letter + -ed.
items.hd: Die meisten: -ed. Endet auf -e: -d. Kurz KVK: letzten Buchstaben verdoppeln + -ed.
items.e: walk→walked (-ed), like→liked (-d), stop→stopped (double p + -ed).
items.ed: walk→walked (-ed), like→liked (-d), stop→stopped (p verdoppeln + -ed).

items.p: Sort: the -ed ending sounds like /t/, /d/, or /ɪd/?
items.h: -ed after voiceless sounds = /t/. After voiced = /d/. After t or d = /ɪd/.
items.hd: -ed nach stimmlosen Lauten = /t/. Nach stimmhaften = /d/. Nach t oder d = /ɪd/.
items.e: walked /t/, played /d/, wanted /ɪd/.
items.ed: walked /t/, played /d/, wanted /ɪd/.

items.p: Find the pairs: base form ↔ regular past form.
items.h: Regular verbs add -ed (or -d, or double + -ed).
items.hd: Regelmaessige Verben bekommen -ed (oder -d, oder verdoppelt + -ed).
items.e: walk→walked, like→liked, stop→stopped, study→studied.
items.ed: walk→walked, like→liked, stop→stopped, study→studied.

items.p: Past form of "play":
items.h: Regular verb — just add -ed.
items.hd: Regelmaessiges Verb — einfach -ed anhaengen.
items.e: play → played (regular: add -ed).
items.ed: play → played (regelmaessig: -ed anhaengen).

items.p: Past form of "stop":
items.h: Short verbs: consonant-vowel-consonant → double last letter + -ed.
items.hd: Kurze Verben: KVK → letzten Buchstaben verdoppeln + -ed.
items.e: stop → stopped (double the p before -ed).
items.ed: stop → stopped (das p wird vor -ed verdoppelt).

items.p: Past form of "study":
items.h: Consonant + y → change y to i, then add -ed.
items.hd: Konsonant + y → y zu i aendern, dann -ed.
items.e: study → studied (y changes to i before -ed).
items.ed: study → studied (y aendert sich zu i vor -ed).

items.p: Sort: which linking word completes the sentence?
items.c: {"and":["I like pizza ___ pasta.|I like pizza and pasta.","She sings ___ dances.|She sings and dances.","Tom ___ Lisa are friends.|Tom and Lisa are friends."],"but":["I like dogs ___ not cats.|I like dogs but not cats.","She is tired ___ happy.|She is tired but happy.","It's old ___ nice.|It's old but nice."],"because":["I stayed home ___ I was ill.|I stayed home because I was ill.","She's happy ___ it's Friday.|She's happy because it's Friday.","We ran ___ it rained.|We ran because it rained."]}
items.h: and = addition. but = contrast. because = reason.
items.hd: and = Ergaenzung. but = Gegensatz. because = Grund.
items.e: and joins similar ideas. but shows contrast. because gives a reason.
items.ed: and verbindet aehnliche Ideen. but zeigt Gegensatz. because gibt einen Grund.

items.p: Find the pairs: base form ↔ past form.
items.h: Irregular verbs do NOT add -ed — each has a unique past form.
items.hd: Unregelmaessige Verben bekommen KEIN -ed — jedes hat eine eigene Vergangenheitsform.
items.e: go→went, see→saw, take→took, have→had, make→made, come→came.
items.ed: go→went, see→saw, take→took, have→had, make→made, come→came.

items.p: Find the pairs: base form ↔ past form.
items.h: These irregular verbs must be learnt by heart.
items.hd: Diese unregelmaessigen Verben muss man auswendig lernen.
items.e: eat→ate, drink→drank, write→wrote, read→read, give→gave, think→thought.
items.ed: eat→ate, drink→drank, write→wrote, read→read, give→gave, think→thought.

items.p: Sort: does this verb form the past with -ed, or is it irregular?
items.h: Regular verbs add -ed. Irregular verbs have special forms.
items.hd: Regelmaessige Verben: -ed. Unregelmaessige: Sonderformen.
items.e: Regular: walked, played. Irregular: went, saw, took.
items.ed: Regelmaessig: walked, played. Unregelmaessig: went, saw, took.

items.p: Past form of "think":
items.h: This is an irregular verb — it does NOT add -ed.
items.hd: Unregelmaessiges Verb — bekommt KEIN -ed.
items.e: think → thought (irregular past form).
items.ed: think → thought (unregelmaessige Vergangenheitsform).

items.p: Past form of "bring":
items.h: This is an irregular verb — similar pattern to think/thought.
items.hd: Unregelmaessiges Verb — aehnliches Muster wie think/thought.
items.e: bring → brought (irregular past form).
items.ed: bring → brought (unregelmaessige Vergangenheitsform).

items.p: Past form of "write":
items.h: This is an irregular verb.
items.hd: Das ist ein unregelmaessiges Verb.
items.e: write → wrote (irregular past form).
items.ed: write → wrote (unregelmaessige Vergangenheitsform).

items.p: Past form of "eat":
items.h: This is an irregular verb — just 3 letters!
items.hd: Unregelmaessiges Verb — nur 3 Buchstaben!
items.e: eat → ate (irregular past form).
items.ed: eat → ate (unregelmaessige Vergangenheitsform).

items.p: Sort: correct or incorrect past negative?
items.c: {"Correct":["I didn't go.","She didn't see it.","We didn't have time.","He didn't play."],"Incorrect":["I didn't went.","She didn't saw it.","We didn't had time.","He didn't played."]}
items.h: didn't + BASE verb (not the past form!). "didn't" already shows past tense.
items.hd: didn't + GRUNDFORM (nicht die Vergangenheitsform!). "didn't" zeigt bereits die Vergangenheit.
items.e: didn't go (correct). didn't went (wrong — base verb after didn't!).
items.ed: didn't go (richtig). didn't went (falsch — Grundform nach didn't!).

items.p: Sort: present continuous (now) or going to (future plan)?
items.c: {"Present Continuous (now)":["I'm eating lunch.","She's reading a book.","They're playing football."],"Going to (future)":["I'm going to eat lunch.","She's going to read a book.","They're going to play football."]}
items.h: Present continuous = happening now. Going to = future plan.
items.hd: Present continuous = passiert jetzt. Going to = Zukunftsplan.
items.e: I'm eating (now). I'm going to eat (future plan).
items.ed: I'm eating (jetzt). I'm going to eat (Zukunftsplan).

items.p: Sort: "am going to", "is going to", or "are going to"?
items.c: {"am going to":["I"],"is going to":["he","she","it"],"are going to":["you","we","they"]}
items.h: Same as "be": I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.hd: Wie bei "be": I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.
items.e: I am going to. He is going to. They are going to.
items.ed: I am going to. He is going to. They are going to.

items.p: Sort: uses "once/twice/three times" or "every"?
items.c: {"once/twice/times":["once a week","twice a day","three times a month","once a year"],"every":["every day","every Monday","every week","every morning"]}
items.h: once/twice/times + a day/week. every + singular noun.
items.hd: once/twice/times + a day/week. every + Nomen im Singular.
items.e: once a week (1x). every day (each day). Both describe frequency.
items.ed: once a week (1x). every day (jeden Tag). Beide beschreiben Haeufigkeit.

items.p: Sort: "How much is" or "How much are"?
items.c: {"How much is":["the book","this pen","a ticket","the pizza"],"How much are":["the shoes","these apples","two tickets","the bananas"]}
items.h: is = singular (one thing). are = plural (more than one).
items.hd: is = Einzahl (eine Sache). are = Mehrzahl (mehr als eins).
items.e: How much is the book? (singular). How much are the shoes? (plural).
items.ed: How much is the book? (Einzahl). How much are the shoes? (Mehrzahl).
